questions.question: What are three common ways to navigate? To navigate successfully, pilots must know their approximate position at all times or be able to determine it whenever they wish. Position may be determined by:
questions.answer: a. Pilotage (by reference to visible landmarks); b. Dead reckoning (by computing direction and distance from a known position); or c. Radio navigation (by use of radio aids).
key_reference_points: Pilotage: navigation by reference to visible landmarks on the ground
Dead reckoning: computing direction and distance from a known position using time, speed, and heading
Radio navigation: using radio aids (VORs, NDBs, GPS) to determine position
Successful navigation requires knowing approximate position at all times

questions.question: What type of aeronautical charts are available for use in VFR navigation? (AIM 9-1-4)
questions.answer: a. Sectional Charts—designed for visual navigation of slow to medium speed aircraft. One inch equals 6.86 nautical miles. They are revised semiannually, except most Alaskan charts which are revised annually. b. VFR Terminal Area Charts (TAC)—TACs depict the Class B airspace. While similar to sectional charts, TACs have more detail because the scale is larger. One inch equals 3.43 nautical miles. Charts are revised semiannually, except in Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands where they are revised annually. c. VFR Flyway Planning Charts—This chart is printed on the reverse side of selected TAC charts. The coverage is the same as the associated TAC. They depict flight paths and altitudes recommended for use to bypass high traffic areas.
key_reference_points: Sectional Charts: 1 inch = 6.86 NM, revised semiannually (Alaska annually)
VFR Terminal Area Charts (TAC): 1 inch = 3.43 NM, depict Class B airspace, revised semiannually
TACs have more detail than sectionals due to larger scale
VFR Flyway Planning Charts: printed on reverse of selected TACs, show recommended flight paths to bypass high traffic areas
Puerto Rico/Virgin Islands TACs revised annually

questions.question: Are electronic flight bags (EFBs) approved for use as a replacement for paper reference material (POH and Supplements, charts, etc.) in the cockpit? (AC 91-78) Yes. EFBs can be used during all phases of flight operations in lieu of paper reference material when the information displayed is the functional equivalent of the paper reference material replaced and is current, up-to-date, and valid. It is recommended that a secondary or back-up source of aeronautical information necessary for the flight be available.
key_reference_points: EFBs are approved for use during all phases of flight per AC 91-78
EFB information must be functionally equivalent to paper material being replaced
EFB data must be current, up-to-date, and valid
A secondary/backup source of aeronautical information is recommended
EFBs can replace POH, supplements, and charts

questions.question: Be capable of locating the following items on a sectional chart: Abandoned airports Air Defense Identification Zone (ADIZ) Airport elevation Airports with a rotating beacon Airports with lighting facilities Airports with services Alert Area Approach Control frequencies ATIS Class B airspace Class C airspace Class D airspace Class D airspace ceiling Class E airspace (controlled airspace 700 foot floor) Class E airspace (controlled airspace 1,200 foot floor) Class E surface area Class E transition area Class G airspace CTAF Flight Service Station frequencies Glider operating area Hard surfaced runway airports HIWAS IFR route Isogonic lines Maximum elevation figures Military Airports Military Operations Area Military Training Routes Mode C veil National Security Area No fixed-wing Special VFR available Non-hard surfaced runways Non-directional radio beacons Non-tower controlled airport Obstructions above 1,000 feet AGL Obstructions below 1,000 feet AGL Parachute Jumping Area Part-time lighting Pilot Controlled Lighting Private airports Prohibited area Restricted area Runway length Special Conservation Area Special Flight Rules Area UNICOM frequencies VFR transition route VFR waypoint Victor airways Visual check points VORTAC Warning area TRSA (Terminal Radar Service Area) if available
key_reference_points: Magenta dashed lines indicate Class E airspace with 700 ft AGL floor
Blue dashed lines indicate Class E airspace with 1,200 ft AGL floor
Solid blue lines indicate Class B airspace; solid magenta lines indicate Class C airspace
Blue segmented circle indicates Class D airspace with ceiling shown in brackets
Mode C veil is 30 NM radius from primary Class B airport requiring transponder with altitude reporting
Magenta flag symbol indicates private airports; blue indicates public airports
Maximum Elevation Figures (MEF) shown in quadrangles as large bold numbers (thousands and hundreds of feet MSL)
Victor airways shown as blue lines between VORs with 'V' designation
Military Training Routes shown as gray lines (IR/VR routes); 4 digits = below 1,500 AGL, 3 digits = above
Obstructions: lightning bolt symbol for lighted, dot for unlighted; bold if above 1,000 ft AGL

questions.question: What is an “isogonic line”? (FAA-H-8083-25) Shown on most aeronautical charts as broken magenta lines, isogonic lines connect points of equal magnetic variation. They show the amount and direction of magnetic variation, which from time to time may vary.
key_reference_points: Isogonic lines are broken magenta lines on aeronautical charts
They connect points of equal magnetic variation
Show both the amount (degrees) and direction (East or West) of variation
Magnetic variation values change slowly over time and are periodically updated
The agonic line is where variation is zero (0°)

questions.question: What is “magnetic variation”? (FAA-H-8083-25) Variation is the angle between true north and magnetic north. It is expressed as east variation or west variation depending upon whether magnetic north (MN) is to the east or west of true north (TN), respectively.
key_reference_points: Magnetic variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north
East variation: Magnetic North is east of True North (subtract from true heading)
West variation: Magnetic North is west of True North (add to true heading)
Memory aid: 'East is Least, West is Best' for converting true to magnetic heading
Variation is shown on sectional charts using isogonic lines
Variation changes based on geographic location

questions.question: How do you convert a true direction to a magnetic direction? (FAA-H- 8083-25) To convert true course or heading to magnetic course or heading, note the variation shown by the nearest isogonic line. If variation is west, add; if east, subtract. Remember: East is Least (Subtract) West is Best (Add)
key_reference_points: Variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north
Isogonic lines on charts show the variation value for an area
East variation: SUBTRACT from true heading (East is Least)
West variation: SUBTRACT from true heading (West is Best)
Formula: True ± Variation = Magnetic
Agonic line marks zero variation (no correction needed)

questions.question: What are lines of latitude and longitude? (FAA-H-8083-25) Circles parallel to the equator (lines running east and west), parallels of latitude, enable us to measure distance in degrees latitude north or south of the equator. Meridians of longitude are drawn from the North Pole to the South Pole and are at right angles to the equator. The “Prime Meridian,” which passes through Greenwich, England, is used as the zero line from which measurements are made in degrees east and west to 180°. The 48 conterminous states of the United States lie between 25 degrees and 49 degrees north latitude and between 67 degrees and 125 degrees west longitude.
key_reference_points: Lines of latitude run east-west, parallel to the equator
Lines of longitude (meridians) run north-south from pole to pole
Latitude measures degrees north or south of equator (0° to 90°)
Longitude measures degrees east or west of Prime Meridian (0° to 180°)
Prime Meridian passes through Greenwich, England
Continental US spans approximately 25°-49°N latitude and 67°-125°W longitude

questions.question: What is “magnetic deviation”? (FAA-H-8083-25) Because of magnetic influences within the airplane itself (electrical circuits, radios, lights, tools, engine, magnetized metal parts, etc.) the compass needle is frequently deflected from its normal reading. This deflection is called deviation. Deviation is different for each airplane, and also varies for different headings of the same airplane. The deviation value may be found on a deviation card located in the airplane.
key_reference_points: Deviation is compass error caused by magnetic interference within the aircraft
Sources include electrical circuits, radios, engines, and magnetized metal parts
Deviation varies by aircraft AND by heading in the same aircraft
Deviation card in cockpit shows correction values for different headings
Deviation is different from variation (which is environmental, not aircraft-specific)
Applied after magnetic heading to get compass heading

questions.question: Name several types of navigational aids. (AIM 1-1-2 through 1-1-7 and 1-1-17)
questions.answer: a. VOR (Very High Frequency Omnidirectional Range) b. VORTAC (VHF Omnidirectional Range/Tactical Air Navigation) c. DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) d. RNAV (Area Navigation) includes INS, VOR/DME-referenced, and GPS.
key_reference_points: VOR - Very High Frequency Omnidirectional Range (ground-based)
VORTAC - VOR combined with military TACAN, provides azimuth and DME
DME - Distance Measuring Equipment, gives slant range distance in NM
RNAV - Area Navigation using GPS, VOR/DME, or INS for point-to-point navigation
Reference AIM 1-1-2 through 1-1-7 and 1-1-17 for detailed NAVAID information

questions.question: What is a “VOR” or “VORTAC”? (FAA-H-8083-25) VORs are VHF radio stations that project radials in all directions (360°) from the station, like spokes from the hub of a wheel. Each of these radials is denoted by its outbound magnetic direction. Almost all VOR stations will also be VORTACs. A VORTAC (VOR-Tactical Air Navigation), provides the standard bearing information of a VOR plus distance information to pilots of airplanes which have distance measuring equipment (DME).
key_reference_points: VOR projects 360 radials outbound from the station like spokes on a wheel
Each radial is identified by its outbound magnetic direction (e.g., R-090 points east)
VORTAC combines VOR azimuth information with TACAN distance (DME) capability
DME requires additional airborne equipment to receive distance information
Most VOR stations are co-located as VORTACs

questions.question: Within what frequency range do VORs operate? (FAA-H-8083-25) Transmitting frequencies of omnirange stations are in the VHF (very high frequency) band between 108 and 117.95 MHz, which are immediately below aviation communication frequencies.
key_reference_points: VOR frequencies range from 108.0 to 117.95 MHz
VOR operates in the VHF (Very High Frequency) band
VOR frequencies are immediately below aviation communication frequencies
Frequencies 108.0-111.95 MHz with odd tenths (108.1, 108.15) are shared with ILS localizers

questions.question: What is a VOR “radial”? (FAA-H-8083-25) A “radial” is defined as a line of magnetic bearing extending from an omnidirectional range (VOR). A VOR projects 360 radials from the station. These radials are always identified by their direction “from” the station. Regardless of heading, an aircraft on the 360° radial will always be located north of the station.
key_reference_points: A radial is a line of magnetic bearing extending outward FROM a VOR station
VOR stations project 360 radials (one for each degree)
Radials are ALWAYS identified by their direction FROM the station, never TO
Aircraft on the 360° radial is always located NORTH of the station
Radial direction is independent of aircraft heading

questions.question: How are VOR NAVAIDs classified? (AIM 1-1-8) Terminal, Low, and High
key_reference_points: Three VOR classifications: Terminal (T), Low (L), and High (H)
Classification determines usable range and altitude limits
Reference: AIM 1-1-8 for VOR NAVAID classifications

questions.question: What reception distances can be expected from the various class VORs? (FAA-H-8083-25) Class Distance/Altitudes Miles T 12,000' and below 25 L Below 18,000' 40 H Below 18,000' 40 H 14,500 – 17,999' 100 (conterminous 48 states only) H 18,000 – FL450 130 H Above FL450 100
key_reference_points: Terminal (T-VOR): 25 NM at 12,000' and below
Low (L-VOR): 40 NM below 18,000'
High (H-VOR) below 18,000': 40 NM
High (H-VOR) 14,500-17,999': 100 NM (conterminous 48 states only)
High (H-VOR) 18,000'-FL450: 130 NM
High (H-VOR) above FL450: 100 NM

questions.question: What limitations, if any, apply to VOR reception distances? (AIM 1- 1-3) VORs are subject to line-of-sight restrictions, and the range varies proportionally to the altitude of the receiving equipment.
key_reference_points: VOR signals are line-of-sight only
Reception range increases with higher altitude
Signal can be blocked by terrain or obstacles
Range is proportional to aircraft altitude above terrain

questions.question: What are the different methods for checking the accuracy of VOR receiver equipment?
questions.answer: a. VOT check—plus or minus 4° b. Ground checkpoint—plus or minus 4° c. Airborne checkpoint—plus or minus 6° d. Dual VOR check—4° between each other e. Selected radial over a known ground point—plus or minus 6°
key_reference_points: VOT check: ±4° tolerance
Ground checkpoint: ±4° tolerance
Airborne checkpoint: ±6° tolerance
Dual VOR check: within 4° of each other
Selected radial over known ground point: ±6° tolerance
Reference: 14 CFR 91.171 for VOR equipment check requirements

questions.question: What is “DME”? (AIM 1-1-7) Distance Measuring Equipment (airborne and ground)—used to measure, in nautical miles, the slant range distance of an aircraft from the DME navigational aid. Aircraft equipped with DME are provided with distance and ground speed information when receiving a VORTAC or TACAN facility. DME operates on frequencies in the UHF spectrum between 960 MHz and 1215 MHz.
key_reference_points: DME stands for Distance Measuring Equipment
Measures slant range distance in nautical miles
Provides distance and groundspeed information
Works with VORTAC or TACAN facilities
Operates on UHF frequencies between 960-1215 MHz
Slant range error greatest at high altitudes close to station

questions.question: Give a brief explanation of GPS. (AIM 1-1-17) Global positioning system (GPS) is a satellite-based radio navigation system that broadcasts a signal used by receivers to determine a precise position anywhere in the world. The receiver tracks multiple satellites and determines a pseudo-range measurement that is then used to determine the user’s location.
key_reference_points: GPS is a satellite-based radio navigation system
Receiver tracks multiple satellites simultaneously
Uses pseudo-range measurements to determine precise position
Provides positioning capability anywhere in the world
Reference: AIM 1-1-17

questions.question: What are the three functional elements of GPS? (FAA-H-8083-15) Space element—consists of 30 satellites. Control element—consists of a network of ground-based GPS monitoring and control stations that ensure the accuracy of satellite positions and their clocks. User element—consists of antennas and receiver-processors onboard aircraft that provide positioning, velocity, and precise timing to the user. 21 What are the different types of GPS receivers available for use? (AIM 1-1-17) GPS receivers used for VFR navigation vary from fully integrated IFR/VFR installations used to support VFR operations, to handheld devices. Pilots must understand the limitations of the receivers prior to using in flight to avoid misusing navigation information.
key_reference_points: Space element: 30 satellites in constellation
Control element: ground-based monitoring and control stations ensuring satellite accuracy
User element: antennas and receiver-processors onboard aircraft
GPS receivers range from fully integrated IFR/VFR installations to handheld devices
Pilots must understand receiver limitations before flight use
Handheld GPS is approved for VFR use only, not primary IFR navigation

questions.question: What is the purpose of RAIM? (FAA-H-8083-6) Receiver autonomous integrity monitoring (RAIM) is a self-monitoring function performed by a GPS receiver to ensure that adequate GPS signals are being received from the satellites at all times. The GPS will alert the pilot whenever the integrity monitoring determines that the GPS signals do not meet the criteria for safe navigational use.
key_reference_points: RAIM = Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring
Self-monitoring function performed by the GPS receiver
Ensures adequate GPS signals are being received from satellites
Alerts pilot when GPS signals don't meet criteria for safe navigation
RAIM prediction should be checked during preflight for IFR operations
Requires minimum of 5 satellites for RAIM, 6 for fault detection and exclusion

questions.question: Where can a pilot obtain RAIM availability information? (AIM 1-1- 17) Pilots may obtain GPS RAIM availability information by using a manufacturer-supplied RAIM prediction tool, or using the Service Availability Prediction Tool (SAPT) on the FAA enroute and terminal RAIM prediction website. Pilots can also request GPS RAIM aeronautical information from an FSS during preflight briefings.
key_reference_points: Manufacturer-supplied RAIM prediction tool can be used for RAIM availability
FAA Service Availability Prediction Tool (SAPT) website provides RAIM predictions
FSS can provide GPS RAIM aeronautical information during preflight briefings
RAIM availability should be checked for both enroute and terminal phases

questions.question: If RAIM capability is lost in-flight, can you continue to use GPS for navigation? (FAA-H-8083-25, AIM 1-1-17) Without RAIM capability, the pilot has no assurance of the accuracy of the GPS position. VFR GPS panel-mount receivers and handheld units have no RAIM alerting capability. This prevents the pilot from being alerted to the loss of the required number of satellites in view, or the detection of a position error.
key_reference_points: Without RAIM, there is no assurance of GPS position accuracy
VFR GPS panel-mount receivers have NO RAIM alerting capability
Handheld GPS units have NO RAIM alerting capability
Loss of RAIM means pilot won't be alerted to insufficient satellites or position errors
Cannot rely on GPS for navigation without RAIM capability

questions.question: Before conducting a flight using GPS equipment for navigation, what basic preflight checks should be made? (FAA-H-8083-16)
questions.answer: a. Verify that the GPS equipment is properly installed and certified for the planned operation. b. Verify that the databases (navigation, terrain, obstacle, etc.) have not expired. c. Review GPS NOTAM/RAIM information related to the planned route of flight. d. Review operational status of ground-based NAVAIDs and related aircraft equipment (e.g., 30-day VOR check) appropriate to route of flight. e. Determine that the GPS receiver operation manual or airplane flight manual supplement is onboard and available for use.
key_reference_points: Verify GPS is properly installed and certified for the planned operation
Check that navigation, terrain, and obstacle databases are NOT expired
Review GPS NOTAMs and RAIM information for planned route
Verify operational status of ground-based NAVAIDs (backup navigation)
Ensure 30-day VOR check is current if VOR will be used
GPS receiver manual or AFM supplement must be onboard and available

questions.question: How can a pilot determine what type of operation a GPS receiver is approved for? (FAA-H-8083-6) The pilot should reference the POH/AFM and supplements to determine the limitations and operating procedures for the particular GPS equipment installed. Most systems require that the avionics operations manual/handbook be on board as a limitation of use.
key_reference_points: Check POH/AFM and supplements for GPS limitations and operating procedures
Avionics operations manual/handbook must typically be on board during flight
GPS approval type (VFR, IFR en route, approach) is specified in aircraft documentation
Equipment limitations are aircraft-specific, not universal

questions.question: During a preflight briefing, will the FSS briefer automatically provide a pilot with GPS NOTAMS? (FAA-H-8083-6) No. You must specifically request GPS/WAAS NOTAMs.
key_reference_points: GPS/WAAS NOTAMs are NOT automatically provided in standard briefings
Pilot must specifically request GPS NOTAMs from FSS
Always ask for GPS NOTAMs when planning to use GPS navigation
NOTAMs may include satellite outages affecting GPS accuracy

questions.question: How many satellites does a GPS receiver require to compute its position? (FAA-H-8083-15) 3 satellites—yields a latitude and longitude position only (2D) 4 satellites—yields latitude, longitude, and altitude position (3D) 5 satellites—3D and RAIM 6 satellites—3D and RAIM (isolates corrupt signal and removes from navigation solution)
key_reference_points: 3 satellites = 2D position (latitude and longitude only)
4 satellites = 3D position (latitude, longitude, and altitude)
5 satellites = 3D position plus RAIM capability
6 satellites = 3D, RAIM, and ability to isolate/remove corrupt signals
RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) requires minimum 5 satellites

questions.question: What is WAAS? (FAA-H-8083-6) The wide area augmentation system (WAAS) is a ground and satellite integrated navigational error correction system that provides accuracy enhancements to signals received from the global positioning system. WAAS provides extremely accurate lateral and vertical navigation signals to aircraft equipped with GPS/WAAS-enabled certified (TSO C-146) equipment.
key_reference_points: WAAS = Wide Area Augmentation System
Ground and satellite integrated system that corrects GPS navigational errors
Provides enhanced lateral AND vertical navigation signals
Requires TSO C-146 certified GPS/WAAS equipment
Enables extremely accurate position information beyond standard GPS

questions.question: What limitations should you be aware of when using a panel-mount VFR GPS or a hand-held VFR GPS system for navigation? (AIM 1-1-17)
questions.answer: a. RAIM capability—Many VFR GPS receivers and all hand-held units have no RAIM alerting capability. Loss of the required number of satellites in view, or the detection of a position error, cannot be displayed to the pilot by such receivers. b. Database currency—In many receivers, an updatable database is used for navigation fixes, airports, and instrument procedures. These databases must be maintained to the current update for IFR operation, but no such requirement exists for VFR use. c. Antenna location—In many VFR installations of GPS receivers, antenna location is more a matter of convenience than performance. Handheld GPS receiver antenna location is limited to the cockpit or cabin only and is rarely optimized to provide a clear view of available satellites. Loss of signal, coupled with a lack of RAIM capability, could present erroneous position and navigation information with no warning to the pilot.
key_reference_points: Many VFR GPS and all handheld units lack RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) alerting capability
No RAIM means no warning if satellites are lost or position errors occur
VFR GPS databases have no currency requirement (unlike IFR which must be current)
Handheld GPS antennas are limited to cockpit placement, not optimized for satellite visibility
Poor antenna location combined with no RAIM can cause erroneous position with no pilot warning

questions.question: Define the term “VFR waypoint.” (FAA-H-8083-25) VFR waypoints provide pilots with a supplementary tool to assist with position awareness while navigating visually in aircraft equipped with area navigation receivers (such as GPS). They provide navigational aids for pilots unfamiliar with an area, waypoint definition of existing reporting points, enhanced navigation in and around Class B and Class C airspace, and around special use airspace. VFR waypoint names consist of a five-letter identifier beginning with “VP” and are retrievable from navigation databases; they should be used only when operating under VFR conditions.
key_reference_points: VFR waypoints are supplementary navigation aids for position awareness during visual flight
Five-letter identifier always begins with 'VP' (e.g., VPXXX)
Retrievable from GPS/RNAV navigation databases
Used for navigation around Class B, Class C, and special use airspace
Should ONLY be used when operating under VFR conditions

questions.question: Before attempting a cross-country flight, a pilot will need to know how to make common calculations for time, speed, distance, amount of fuel required, as well as basic wind calculations. Solve the following: Time, speed and distance problems:
questions.answer: a. If time equals 25 minutes and distance equals 47 NM, what will speed be? b. If distance equals 84 NM and speed equals 139 knots, what will time be? c. If speed is 85 knots and time is 51 minutes, what will the distance be? a. 113 knots b. 36 minutes c. 72 NM Fuel consumption problems: a. If gallons-per-hour is 9.3 and time is 1 hour, 27 minutes, how many gallons will be consumed? b. If time is 2 hours, 13 minutes and gallons consumed is 32, what will the gallons-per-hour be? c. If gallons consumed is 38 and gallons-per-hour is 10.8, what will the time be? a. 13.5 gallons b. 14.4 GPH c. 3 hours, 31 minutes True airspeed problems: a. If altitude is 10,000 feet, temperature is 0°C, and IAS is 115, what will the TAS be? b. If IAS is 103, altitude is 6,000 feet, and the temperature is -10°C, what will the TAS be? c. If the temperature is 40°F, the IAS is 115, and the altitude is 11,000 feet, what will the TAS be? a. 135 TAS b. 110 TAS c. 139 TAS Density altitude problems: a. If pressure altitude is 1,500 feet and the temperature is 35°C, what will the density altitude be? b. If pressure altitude is 5,000 feet and the temperature is -10°C, what will the density altitude be? c. If the pressure altitude is 2,000 feet and the temperature is 30°C, what will the density altitude be? a. 4,100 feet b. 3,100 feet c. 4,200 feet Conversion problems: a. 100 nautical miles = ______ statute miles b. 12 quarts oil = ______ pounds c. 45 gallons fuel = ______ pounds d. 80°F = ______ °C e. 20 knots = ______ miles per hour a. 115 SM b. 22.5 pounds c. 270 pounds d. 26.6°C e. 23 MPH Ground speed/true heading problems: a. If wind direction is 220, wind speed is 030, true course is 146, and TAS is 135, what will ground speed and true heading be? b. If wind direction is 240, wind speed is 025, true course is 283 and TAS is 165, what will ground speed and true heading be? c. If wind direction is 060, wind speed is 030, true course is 036 and TAS is 140, what will ground speed and true heading be? a. Ground speed is 124, true heading is 158. b. Ground speed is 146, true heading is 277. c. Ground speed is 112, true heading is 041.
key_reference_points: Speed = Distance ÷ Time (in hours); Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance ÷ Speed
Fuel consumed = GPH × Time (in hours); GPH = Gallons ÷ Time; Time = Gallons ÷ GPH
TAS increases approximately 2% per 1,000 feet of altitude above standard temperature
Density altitude increases approximately 120 feet per 1°C above standard temperature
Conversions: 1 NM = 1.15 SM; 1 gallon avgas = 6 lbs; 1 quart oil = 1.875 lbs; °C = (°F - 32) × 5/9; 1 knot = 1.15 MPH
Use E6B or electronic flight computer for wind correction angle (WCA) and ground speed calculations

questions.question: Flight log example, VFR flight plan: Careful preflight planning is extremely important. A wise pilot ensures a successful cross-country flight by getting a good preflight briefing, completing a flight log, and filing a flight plan before flight.
questions.answer: a. Get a preflight briefing consisting of the latest or most current weather, airport, and enroute NAVAID information. b. Draw course lines and mark checkpoints on the chart. c. Enter checkpoints on the log. d. Enter NAVAIDs on the log. e. Enter VOR courses on the log. f. Enter altitude on the log. g. Enter the wind (direction/velocity) and temperature on the log. h. Measure the true course on the chart and enter it on the log. i. Compute the true airspeed and enter it on the log. j. Compute the WCA and GS and enter them on the log. k. Determine variation from chart and enter it on the log. l. Determine deviation from compass correction card and enter it on the log. m. Enter compass heading on the log. n. Measure distances on the chart and enter them on the log. o. Figure ETE and ETA and enter them on the log. p. Calculate fuel burn and usage; enter them on the log. q. Compute weight and balance. r. Compute takeoff and landing performance. s. Complete a Flight Plan form. t. File the Flight Plan with FSS.
key_reference_points: Always obtain a standard weather briefing from FSS or approved sources before cross-country flight
Flight log sequence: checkpoints → NAVAIDs → courses → wind/temp → TAS → WCA/GS → variation → deviation → compass heading
True Heading = True Course ± Wind Correction Angle (WCA)
Magnetic Heading = True Heading ± Variation (East is least, West is best)
Compass Heading = Magnetic Heading ± Deviation (from compass correction card)
Complete weight and balance AND takeoff/landing performance calculations before every flight
File VFR flight plan with FSS; it does not activate automatically—pilot must open it after departure

questions.question: After takeoff, you attempt to activate you VFR flight plan but are unable to contact the FSS. What will happen to your filed flight plan? (FAA-H-8083-25) When a VFR flight plan is filed, it is held by the FSS until 1 hour after the proposed departure time and then is canceled.
key_reference_points: VFR flight plans are held by FSS for only 1 hour after proposed departure time, then automatically canceled
Pilot is responsible for opening (activating) VFR flight plan after departure
VFR flight plans can be opened by radio with FSS or by phone before departure
If unable to contact FSS, try another frequency, use 122.2 (universal FSS frequency), or call by phone
Unlike IFR flight plans, VFR flight plans do not activate automatically through ATC

questions.question: Diversion to Alternate/Lost Procedures:
questions.answer: a. What actions should be taken if you become disoriented or lost on a cross-country flight (no GPS available)? Condition I: plenty of fuel and weather conditions good. • Straighten up and fly right. Fly a specific heading in a direction you believe to be correct (or circle, if unsure); don’t wander aimlessly. • If you have been flying a steady compass heading and keeping a relatively accurate navigation log, it’s not likely you will have a problem locating your position. • If several VORs are within reception distance, use them for a cross- bearing to determine position (even a single VOR can be of enormous help in narrowing down your possible position); or, fly to the station—there’s no doubt where you are then. • Use knowledge of your last known position, elapsed time, approximate wind direction and ground speed, to establish how far you may have traveled since your last checkpoint. • Use this distance as a radius and draw a semicircle ahead of your last known position on chart. For example, you estimate your ground speed at 120 knots. If you have been flying 20 minutes since your last checkpoint, then the no-wind radius of your semicircle is 40 miles projected along the direction of your estimated track. • If still unsure of your position, loosen up the eyeballs and start some first- class pilotage. Look for something big. Don’t concern yourself with the minute or trivial at this point. Often, there will be linear features such as rivers, mountain ranges, or prominent highways and railroads that are easy to identify. You can use them simply as references for orientation purposes and thus find them of great value in fixing your approximate position. Condition II: low on fuel; weather deteriorating; inadequate experience; darkness imminent; and/or equipment malfunctioning. Get it on the ground! Most accidents are the product of mistakes which have multiplied over a period of time and getting lost is no exception: don’t push your luck. It may well be that in doing so, you have added the final mistake which will add another figure to the accident statistics. If terrain or other conditions make landing impossible at the moment, don’t waste time, for it is of the essence: don’t search for the perfect field—anything usable will do. Remember, most people on the ground know where they are, and you know that you do not. b. If it becomes apparent that you cannot locate your position, what action is recommended at this point? The FAA recommends the use of the “4 Cs”: • Climb—The higher altitude allows better communication capability as well as better visual range for identification of landmarks. • Communicate—Use the system. Use 121.5 MHz if no other frequency produces results. It is guarded by FSS’s, control towers, military towers, approach control facilities, and Air Route Traffic Control Centers. • Confess—Once communications are established, let them know your problem. • Comply—Follow instructions. c. While en route on a cross-country flight, weather has deteriorated and it has become necessary to divert to an alternate airport. Assuming no GPS or DME capability, describe how you will navigate to the alternate. (FAA-H-8083-25) • After selecting my alternate, I will approximate the magnetic course to the alternate using a straight edge and a compass rose from a nearby VOR or an airway that closely parallels my direction to the alternate. • I can use the straight edge and scale at the bottom of the chart to approximate a distance to the alternate. I can fine-tune this course and distance later, as time permits, with a plotter. • If time permits, I’ll start my diversion over a prominent ground feature. However, in an emergency, I will divert promptly toward my alternate.
key_reference_points: Condition I (good fuel/weather): Fly a specific heading, use VORs for cross-bearing position fix, calculate semicircle from last known position using groundspeed and elapsed time
Condition II (low fuel/deteriorating weather/darkness): Get on the ground immediately - don't search for perfect field
The 4 Cs when lost: Climb (better comm/visibility), Communicate (use 121.5 if needed), Confess (tell ATC your problem), Comply (follow instructions)
121.5 MHz is guarded by FSS, towers, military towers, approach control, and ARTCCs
Diversion technique: Use nearby VOR compass rose or parallel airway to estimate magnetic course, use chart scale for distance approximation
Look for prominent linear features (rivers, highways, railroads, mountain ranges) to establish position
questions.note: Attempting to complete all plotting, measuring, and computations involved before diverting to the alternate destination may only aggravate an actual emergency. • Once established on course, I’ll note the time, and then use the winds aloft nearest to my diversion point to calculate a heading and GS. Once I have my GS, I’ll determine my ETA and fuel consumption to the alternate. • I’ll give priority to flying the aircraft while dividing attention between navigation and planning. • When determining my altitude to use while diverting, I’ll consider cloud heights, winds, terrain, and radio reception.

questions.question: What is the most common type of communication radio equipment installed in general aviation aircraft? How many channels are available? (FAA-H-8083-25) In general aviation, the most common types of radios are VHF. A VHF radio operates on frequencies between 118.0 and 136.975 MHz and is classified as 720 or 760 depending on the number of channels it can accommodate. The 720 and 760 uses .025 spacing (118.025, 118.050, etc.) with the 720 having a frequency range up to 135.975 and the 760 going up to 136.975.
key_reference_points: VHF radios operate between 118.0 and 136.975 MHz
720-channel radios have frequency range up to 135.975 MHz
760-channel radios have frequency range up to 136.975 MHz
Channel spacing is .025 MHz (e.g., 118.025, 118.050)
VHF is the most common communication radio in general aviation aircraft

questions.question: What is the universal VHF “Emergency” frequency? (AIM 5-6-2 and 6-3-1) 121.5 MHz; this frequency is guarded by military towers, most civil towers, FSS’s, and radar facilities.
key_reference_points: 121.5 MHz is the universal VHF emergency frequency
121.5 MHz is guarded by military towers, most civil towers, FSS, and radar facilities
Use 121.5 MHz when no other frequency produces results
questions.note: All aircraft operating in the U.S. national airspace are highly encouraged to maintain a listening watch on VHF/UHF guard frequencies (121.5 or 243.0 MHz).

questions.question: What frequencies are used for ground control? (AIM 4-3-14) The majority of ground control frequencies are 121.6 to 121.9 MHz.
key_reference_points: Ground control frequencies range from 121.6 to 121.9 MHz
Ground control manages aircraft movement on taxiways and ramp areas
These frequencies are separate from tower frequencies (typically 118.0-121.5 MHz)

questions.question: What is a “CTAF”? (AIM 4-1-9) A CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) is a frequency designated for the purpose of carrying out airport advisory practices while operating to or from an airport without an operating control tower. The CTAF may be a UNICOM, MULTICOM, FSS or TOWER frequency and is identified in appropriate aeronautical publications.
key_reference_points: CTAF stands for Common Traffic Advisory Frequency
Used at airports without an operating control tower
CTAF can be a UNICOM, MULTICOM, FSS, or tower frequency
CTAF is identified in Chart Supplement (formerly A/FD) and sectional charts
All traffic advisories are self-announced on CTAF

questions.question: What is “UNICOM,” and what frequencies are designated for its use? (AIM 4-1-9 and 4-1-11) UNICOM is a nongovernment communication facility which may provide airport information at certain airports. Airports other than those with a control tower/FSS on airport will normally use 122.700, 122.725, 122.800, 122.975, 123.000, 123.050, and 123.075 MHz. Airports with a control tower or an FSS on airport will normally use 122.950 MHz.
key_reference_points: UNICOM is a nongovernment communication facility
Provides airport information (wind, runway in use, traffic) at certain airports
Airports with tower or FSS on field use 122.950 MHz for UNICOM
UNICOM is advisory only — not ATC instructions

questions.question: What does “ATIS” mean? (AIM 4-1-13) Automatic Terminal Information Service (ATIS) is the continuous broadcast of recorded noncontrol information in selected high-activity terminal areas. Its purpose is to improve controller effectiveness and to relieve frequency congestion by automating the repetitive transmission of essential but routine information.
key_reference_points: ATIS = Automatic Terminal Information Service
Continuous broadcast of recorded NON-CONTROL information
Found at high-activity terminal areas
Purpose: improve controller effectiveness and reduce frequency congestion
Contains routine but essential information (weather, runways, NOTAMs)
Each ATIS broadcast is identified by a phonetic letter (Alpha, Bravo, etc.)

questions.question: If operating into an airport without an operating control tower, FSS or UNICOM, what procedure should be followed? (AIM 4-1-9, Glossary) Where there is no tower, FSS, or UNICOM station on the airport, use MULTICOM frequency 122.9 for self-announce procedures. MULTICOM is a mobile service not open to public use, used to provide communications essential to conduct the activities being performed by or directed from private aircraft.
key_reference_points: Use MULTICOM frequency 122.9 when no tower, FSS, or UNICOM available
MULTICOM is for self-announce procedures
MULTICOM is a mobile service NOT open to public use
Used for communications essential to private aircraft operations
Announce position, intentions, and traffic pattern information on 122.9

questions.question: What frequencies are monitored by most FSS’s other than 121.5? (AIM 4-2-14) FSS’s and supplemental weather service locations (SWSL) have assigned frequencies for their different functions. For example, in Alaska, certain FSS’s provide local airport advisory on 123.6 MHz, or other frequencies that can be found in the Chart Supplement U.S. If in doubt about what to use, 122.2 MHz is designated as a common enroute simplex frequency at most FSS’s.
key_reference_points: 122.2 MHz is the common enroute simplex frequency at most FSS's
FSS's monitor 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency)
In Alaska, some FSS's provide Local Airport Advisory on 123.6 MHz
Specific FSS frequencies are listed in the Chart Supplement U.S.
FSS's have different assigned frequencies for different functions
questions.exam_tip: Be prepared to explain how you will obtain updated weather information while en route. Know how to contact an FSS along your route of flight.

questions.question: What is an RCO? (AIM Glossary) A remote communications outlet (RCO) is an unmanned communications facility remotely controlled by ATC personnel, established for the purpose of providing ground-to-ground communications between ATC and pilots located at satellite airports. ATC may use the RCO to deliver en route clearances and departure authorizations, and to acknowledge IFR cancellations or departure/landing times. As a secondary function, RCOs may be used for advisory purposes whenever the aircraft is below the coverage of the primary air/ground frequency.
key_reference_points: RCO stands for Remote Communications Outlet
RCO is an unmanned communications facility remotely controlled by ATC
Primary purpose: ground-to-ground communication between ATC and pilots at satellite airports
Used for en route clearances, departure authorizations, and IFR cancellations
Secondary function: advisory purposes when aircraft is below primary frequency coverage

questions.question: How can a pilot determine what frequency is appropriate for activating his/her VFR flight plan once airborne? Two ways:
questions.answer: a. Ask the FSS briefer during the preflight weather briefing. b. Consult the communications section under flight service for the airport of departure in the Chart Supplement U.S.
key_reference_points: Ask the FSS briefer during preflight weather briefing for activation frequency
Consult Chart Supplement U.S. communications section under flight service for departure airport
Flight plans can be activated once airborne by contacting FSS on appropriate frequency
122.2 MHz is a common FSS frequency for flight plan activation

questions.question: What is the meaning of a heavy-lined blue box surrounding a NAVAID frequency? (Chart Legend) A heavy-lined blue box surrounding the NAVAID station data indicates FSS frequencies 121.5, 122.2, 243.0 and 255.4 are available.
key_reference_points: Heavy-lined blue box surrounds NAVAID frequency data on sectional charts
Indicates FSS frequencies are available at that location
Standard FSS frequencies available: 121.5, 122.2, 243.0, and 255.4 MHz
121.5 MHz is the emergency frequency
Found in Chart Legend for reference

questions.question: Why would a frequency be printed on top of a heavy-lined box? (Chart Legend) This usually means that this frequency is available in addition to the standard FSS frequencies.
key_reference_points: Heavy-lined box indicates FSS (Flight Service Station) communication available
Frequency printed on top means it is available IN ADDITION to standard FSS frequencies (122.2, 122.4, etc.)
Standard FSS frequencies are assumed available unless otherwise noted
Used for obtaining weather briefings, filing flight plans, and pilot reports

questions.question: What is the meaning of a thin-lined blue box surrounding a NAVAID frequency? (Chart Legend) A plain box without frequencies on top indicates that there are no standard FSS frequencies available. These NAVAIDs will have a “no voice” symbol (underline under frequency).
key_reference_points: Thin-lined blue box indicates NO standard FSS frequencies available at that NAVAID
An underline beneath the frequency indicates 'no voice' capability
No voice NAVAIDs provide only navigation signals, not communication
Pilot must use other means to contact FSS when near these NAVAIDs

questions.question: Why would a frequency be printed on top of a thin-lined blue box? (Chart Legend) These frequencies are the best frequencies to use in the immediate vicinity of the NAVAID site, and will ensure reception by the controlling FSS at low altitudes without terrain interference. They will normally be followed by an “R” which indicates that the FSS can receive only on that frequency (you transmit on that frequency). The pilot will listen for a response over the NAVAID frequency.
key_reference_points: Frequency on top of thin-lined box is best for low-altitude communication near that NAVAID
These frequencies minimize terrain interference for FSS reception
'R' suffix means FSS can RECEIVE only on that frequency (pilot transmits on it)
Pilot listens for FSS response over the NAVAID's navigation frequency
This is a duplex communication setup - transmit and receive on different frequencies

questions.question: How can a pilot determine the availability of HIWAS when looking at a VFR Sectional chart? (FAA-H-8083-25) Navaids that have HIWAS capability are depicted on sectional charts with an “H” in the upper right corner of the identification box.
key_reference_points: HIWAS = Hazardous Inflight Weather Advisory Service
Look for letter 'H' in upper right corner of NAVAID identification box
HIWAS provides continuous broadcast of hazardous weather information
Found on VFR Sectional charts near NAVAID frequency boxes

questions.question: What meaning does the letter “T” in a solid blue circle appearing in the top right corner of a NAVAID frequency box have? (Chart Legend) (Alaska only) A Transcribed Weather Broadcast is available. A TWEB is a continuous recording of meteorological and aeronautical information that is broadcast on L/MF and VOR facilities for pilots.
key_reference_points: Letter 'T' in solid blue circle indicates TWEB availability
TWEB = Transcribed Weather Broadcast
Alaska only feature on sectional charts
TWEB broadcasts continuous meteorological and aeronautical information
Transmitted on L/MF and VOR facilities

questions.question: If an inflight emergency requires immediate action by the pilot, what authority and responsibilities does he/she have?
questions.answer: a. The PIC is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of that aircraft. b. In an inflight emergency requiring immediate action, the PIC may deviate from any rule in Part 91 to the extent required to meet that emergency. c. Each PIC who deviates from a Part 91 rule shall, upon request from the Administrator, send a written report of that deviation to the Administrator.
key_reference_points: 14 CFR 91.3 grants PIC final authority over aircraft operation
PIC may deviate from ANY Part 91 rule during emergency requiring immediate action
Deviation allowed only to extent required to meet the emergency
Must submit written report to Administrator upon request
PIC is directly responsible for aircraft operation

questions.question: What restrictions apply to pilots concerning the use of drugs and alcohol? (14 CFR 91.17) No person may act or attempt to act as a crewmember of a civil aircraft:
questions.answer: a. within 8 hours after the consumption of any alcoholic beverage; b. while under the influence of alcohol; c. while using any drug that affects the person’s faculties in any way contrary to safety; or d. while having an alcohol concentration of .04 percent or more in a blood or breath specimen.
key_reference_points: 8 hours 'bottle to throttle' rule - no alcohol within 8 hours of flying
Blood alcohol concentration must be below 0.04%
No flying while under the influence of alcohol regardless of time elapsed
No drugs that affect faculties contrary to safety
These rules apply to all crewmembers, not just pilots

questions.question: Is it permissible for a pilot to allow a person who is obviously under the influence of intoxicating liquors or drugs to be carried aboard an aircraft? (14 CFR 91.17) No. Except in an emergency, no pilot of a civil aircraft may allow a person who appears to be intoxicated or who demonstrates by manner or physical indications that the individual is under the influence of drugs (except a medical patient under proper care) to be carried in that aircraft.
key_reference_points: Cannot carry passengers who appear intoxicated or under influence of drugs
Exception exists for emergency situations
Exception for medical patients under proper care
Applies to both alcohol intoxication and drug influence
Determination based on appearance, manner, or physical indications

questions.question: May portable electronic devices be operated onboard an aircraft? (14 CFR 91.21) Aircraft operated by a holder of an air carrier operating certificate or an aircraft operating under IFR may not allow operation of electronic devices onboard their aircraft. Exceptions are: portable voice recorders, hearing aids, heart pacemakers, electric shavers, or any other device that the operator of the aircraft has determined will not cause interference with the navigation or communication system of the aircraft on which it is to be used.
key_reference_points: Electronic device restrictions apply to air carrier operations and IFR flights
Permitted devices: portable voice recorders, hearing aids, heart pacemakers, electric shavers
Operator may approve devices determined not to interfere with navigation/communication systems
Part 91 VFR operations have more flexibility with electronic devices
Interference with aircraft systems is the primary concern

questions.question: Under what conditions may objects be dropped from an aircraft? (14 CFR 91.15) No pilot-in-command of a civil aircraft may allow any object to be dropped from that aircraft in flight that creates a hazard to persons or property. However, this section does not prohibit the dropping of any object if reasonable precautions are taken to avoid injury or damage to persons or property.
key_reference_points: 14 CFR 91.15 governs dropping objects from aircraft
No object may be dropped that creates a hazard to persons or property
Dropping is permitted if reasonable precautions are taken to avoid injury or damage
The PIC is responsible for ensuring compliance

questions.question: Concerning a flight in the local area, is any preflight action required, and if so, what must it consist of? (14 CFR 91.103) Yes, pilots must familiarize themselves with all available information concerning that flight, including runway lengths at airports of intended use, and takeoff and landing distance data under existing conditions.
key_reference_points: 14 CFR 91.103 requires preflight action for ALL flights, including local
Must familiarize with all available information concerning the flight
Runway lengths at airports of intended use must be reviewed
Takeoff and landing distance data under existing conditions must be known
This applies even for flights remaining in the local area

questions.question: Preflight action as required by regulation for all flights away from the vicinity of the departure airport shall include a review of what specific information? (14 CFR 91.103) For a flight under IFR or a flight not in the vicinity of an airport— N OTAMs W eather reports and forecasts K nown ATC traffic delays R unway lengths at airports of intended use A lternatives available if the planned flight cannot be completed F uel requirements
key_reference_points: 14 CFR 91.103 applies to IFR flights or flights not in vicinity of departure airport
Memory aid NWKRAF: NOTAMs, Weather, Known ATC delays, Runway lengths, Alternatives, Fuel requirements
Must review weather reports and forecasts
Must check NOTAMs for route and destination
Must know fuel requirements for the flight
Must have alternatives if planned flight cannot be completed

questions.question: Which persons on board an aircraft are required to use seatbelts and when? (14 CFR 91.107) Each person on board a U.S.-registered civil aircraft must occupy an approved seat or berth with a safety belt, and if installed, shoulder harness, properly secured about him or her during movement on the surface, takeoff and landing. However a person who has not reached his or her second birthday and does not occupy or use any restraining device may be held by an adult who is occupying a seat or berth, and a person on board for the purpose of engaging in sport parachuting may use the floor of the aircraft as a seat.
key_reference_points: Seatbelts required during surface movement, takeoff, and landing
Shoulder harness must also be used if installed
Children under 2 years old may be held by an adult without a restraint
Sport parachutists may use the aircraft floor as a seat
Must occupy an approved seat or berth with safety belt properly secured

questions.question: What responsibility does the pilot-in-command have concerning passengers and their use of seatbelts? (14 CFR 91.107) No pilot may take off a U.S. registered civil aircraft unless the pilot-in- command of that aircraft ensures that each person on board is briefed on how to fasten and unfasten that person’s safety belt and shoulder harness, if installed. The pilot-in-command shall ensure that all persons on board have been notified to fasten their seatbelt and shoulder harness, if installed, before movement of the aircraft on the surface, takeoff or landing.
key_reference_points: PIC must brief all passengers on how to fasten AND unfasten seatbelts/shoulder harnesses
PIC must notify all persons to fasten seatbelts before surface movement, takeoff, or landing
Briefing requirement includes shoulder harness if installed
Cannot take off until briefing is complete

questions.question: When are flight crewmembers required to keep their seatbelts and shoulder harnesses fastened? (14 CFR 91.105) During takeoff and landing, and while en route, each required flight crewmember shall keep his/her seatbelt fastened while at his/her station. During takeoff and landing this includes shoulder harnesses, if installed, unless it interferes with other required duties.
key_reference_points: Flight crew must keep seatbelt fastened at their station while en route
Shoulder harness required during takeoff and landing if installed
Shoulder harness exception: may be unfastened if it interferes with required duties
Applies to each REQUIRED flight crewmember
Seatbelt required at all times at crew station (not just takeoff/landing)

questions.question: If operating an aircraft in close proximity to another, such as formation flight, what regulations apply?
questions.answer: a. No person may operate an aircraft so close to another aircraft as to create a collision hazard. b. No person may operate an aircraft in formation flight except by arrangement with the pilot-in-command of each aircraft in the formation. c. No person may operate an aircraft, carrying passengers for hire, in formation flight.
key_reference_points: No aircraft may operate so close to another as to create a collision hazard
Formation flight requires prior arrangement with PIC of each aircraft
Formation flight is prohibited when carrying passengers for hire
Reference: 14 CFR 91.111

questions.question: What is the order of right-of-way as applied to the different categories of aircraft? (14 CFR 91.113) B alloons G liders A irships A irplanes R otorcraft Aircraft towing or refueling other aircraft have the right-of-way over all other engine-driven aircraft. Remember: BGAAR (BIG “R”)
key_reference_points: Right-of-way priority order: Balloons, Gliders, Airships, Airplanes, Rotorcraft (BGAAR)
Balloons have highest right-of-way priority among aircraft categories
Aircraft towing or refueling has right-of-way over all other engine-driven aircraft
Less maneuverable aircraft have priority over more maneuverable aircraft
Reference: 14 CFR 91.113

questions.question: When would an aircraft have the right-of-way over all other air traffic? (14 CFR 91.113) An aircraft in distress has the right-of-way over all other air traffic.
key_reference_points: Aircraft in distress has right-of-way over ALL other air traffic
This rule overrides all other right-of-way categories
Applies regardless of aircraft type or category
Reference: 14 CFR 91.113

questions.question: State the required action for each of the aircraft confrontations (same category), below. (14 CFR 91.113) Converging Approaching head-on Overtaking Converging—aircraft on right has the right-of-way. Approaching head-on—both aircraft shall alter course to right. Overtaking—aircraft being overtaken has the right-of-way; pilot of the overtaking aircraft shall alter course to the right.
key_reference_points: Converging: Aircraft on the RIGHT has right-of-way (you give way to your right)
Head-on approach: BOTH aircraft alter course to the RIGHT
Overtaking: Aircraft being overtaken has right-of-way; overtaking aircraft passes on the RIGHT
These rules apply to aircraft in the same category
Reference: 14 CFR 91.113

questions.question: What right-of-way rules apply when two or more aircraft are approaching an airport for the purpose of landing? (14 CFR 91.113) Aircraft on final approach to land or while landing have the right-of-way over aircraft in flight or operating on the surface, except that they shall not take advantage of this rule to force an aircraft off the runway surface which has already landed and is attempting to make way for an aircraft on final approach. When two or more aircraft are approaching an airport for the purpose of landing, the aircraft at the lower altitude has the right-of-way, but it shall not take advantage of this rule to cut in front of another which is on final approach to land or to overtake that aircraft.
key_reference_points: Aircraft on final approach or landing has right-of-way over aircraft in flight or on surface
Cannot force an aircraft off the runway that has already landed
When multiple aircraft approaching to land, LOWER altitude has right-of-way
Lower aircraft cannot cut in front of or overtake aircraft on final approach
Reference: 14 CFR 91.113

questions.question: Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, what is the maximum indicated airspeed at which a person may operate an aircraft below 10,000 feet MSL? (14 CFR 91.117) No person may operate an aircraft below 10,000 feet MSL at an indicated airspeed of more than 250 knots (288 MPH).
key_reference_points: Maximum speed below 10,000 feet MSL: 250 knots (288 MPH)
Speed limit applies unless authorized or required by ATC
This is INDICATED airspeed, not true airspeed
Additional limit: 200 knots within Class D surface area or within 4 NM of Class C/D primary airport below 2,500 AGL
Reference: 14 CFR 91.117

questions.question: What is the minimum safe altitude that an aircraft may be operated over a congested area of a city? (14 CFR 91.119) Except when necessary for takeoff or landing, no person may operate an aircraft over a congested area of a city, town, or settlement, or over any open- air assembly of persons, below an altitude of 1,000 feet above the highest obstacle within a horizontal radius of 2,000 feet of the aircraft.
key_reference_points: Congested areas require 1,000 feet above highest obstacle
Horizontal radius of 2,000 feet from aircraft
Applies over cities, towns, settlements, and open-air assemblies
Exception exists for takeoff and landing operations
Reference: 14 CFR 91.119

questions.question: In areas other than congested areas, what minimum safe altitudes shall be used? (14 CFR 91.119) Except when necessary for takeoff or landing, an aircraft shall be operated no lower than 500 feet above the surface, except over open water or sparsely populated areas. In those cases, the aircraft may not be operated closer than 500 feet to any person, vessel, vehicle or structure.
key_reference_points: Non-congested areas require 500 feet above the surface
Over open water or sparsely populated areas: 500 feet from any person, vessel, vehicle, or structure
Exception exists for takeoff and landing operations
This is a horizontal clearance rule, not altitude above surface
Reference: 14 CFR 91.119

questions.question: Define “minimum safe altitude.” (14 CFR 91.119) An altitude allowing, if a power unit fails, an emergency landing without undue hazard to persons or property on the surface.
key_reference_points: Must allow emergency landing without undue hazard if power fails
Applies to persons and property on the surface
This is the foundational principle behind all 91.119 altitude requirements
Reference: 14 CFR 91.119

questions.question: What is the lowest altitude an aircraft may be operated over an area designated as a U.S. wildlife refuge, park or Forest Service Area? (AIM 7-4-6) All aircraft are requested to maintain a minimum altitude of 2,000 feet above the surface.
key_reference_points: Minimum altitude of 2,000 feet AGL requested over wildlife refuges, parks, and Forest Service Areas
This is a voluntary request, not a regulatory requirement
Reference: AIM 7-4-6

questions.question: When flying below 18,000 feet MSL, cruising altitude must be maintained by reference to an altimeter set using what procedure? (14 CFR 91.121) When the barometric pressure is 31.00" Hg or less, each person operating an aircraft must maintain the cruising altitude of that aircraft by reference to an altimeter that is set to the current reported altimeter setting of a station along the route and within 100 nautical miles of the aircraft. If there is no station within this area, the current reported altimeter setting of an available station may be used. If the barometric pressure exceeds 31.00" Hg, consult the Aeronautical Information Manual for correct procedures.
key_reference_points: Below 18,000 feet MSL, set altimeter to current reported altimeter setting
Station must be along the route and within 100 nautical miles of the aircraft
If no station within 100 NM, use any available station's reported setting
Standard procedure applies when barometric pressure is 31.00" Hg or less
Consult AIM for special procedures when pressure exceeds 31.00" Hg
Reference: 14 CFR 91.121

questions.question: If an altimeter setting is not available before flight, what procedure should be used? (14 CFR 91.121) Use the same procedure as in the case of an aircraft not equipped with a radio: the elevation of the departure airport or an appropriate altimeter setting available before departure should be used.
key_reference_points: If no altimeter setting available, use departure airport elevation
Alternative: use any appropriate altimeter setting obtained before departure
Same procedure applies for aircraft not equipped with a radio
Reference: 14 CFR 91.121

questions.question: When may a pilot intentionally deviate from an ATC clearance or instruction? (14 CFR 91.123) No pilot may deviate from an ATC clearance unless:
questions.answer: a. an amended clearance has been obtained, b. an emergency exists, c. or in response to a traffic and collision avoidance system resolution advisory.
key_reference_points: May deviate only if an amended clearance is obtained
May deviate if an emergency exists
May deviate in response to a TCAS Resolution Advisory (RA)
Reference: 14 CFR 91.123

questions.question: As pilot-in-command, what action, if any, is required of you if you deviate from an ATC instruction and priority is given? (14 CFR 91.123) Two actions are required of you as PIC:
questions.answer: a. Each pilot-in-command who, in an emergency or in response to a traffic alert and collision avoidance system resolution advisory, deviates from an ATC clearance or instruction must notify ATC of that deviation as soon as possible. b. Each pilot-in-command who is given priority by ATC in an emergency shall submit a detailed report of that emergency within 48 hours to the manager of that ATC facility, if requested by ATC (on-the-ground responsibility).
key_reference_points: Must notify ATC of deviation as soon as possible after emergency or TCAS RA
If given priority by ATC, must submit detailed written report within 48 hours IF requested
Report goes to the manager of the ATC facility that gave priority
Two separate obligations: immediate notification AND possible written report
Reference: 14 CFR 91.123

questions.question: In the event of radio failure while operating an aircraft to, from, through or on an airport having an operational tower, what are the different types and meanings of light gun signals you might receive from an ATC tower? (14 CFR 91.125) Light On Ground In Air Steady Green Cleared for Takeoff Cleared to Land Flashing Green Cleared to Taxi Return for Landing Steady Red Stop Yield, Continue Circling Flashing Red Taxi Clear of Runway Unsafe, Do Not Land Flashing White Return to Start Not Used Alternate Exercise Exercise Red/Green Extreme Caution Extreme Caution
key_reference_points: Steady Green: On ground = Cleared for takeoff; In air = Cleared to land
Flashing Green: On ground = Cleared to taxi; In air = Return for landing
Steady Red: On ground = Stop; In air = Yield/Continue circling
Flashing Red: On ground = Taxi clear of runway; In air = Airport unsafe, do not land
Flashing White: On ground = Return to starting point; In air = Not used
Alternating Red/Green: Exercise extreme caution (both ground and air)
Reference: 14 CFR 91.125
questions.note: Most pilots find these hard to remember; always have a reference available in the cockpit (kneeboard, placard, or EFB app).

questions.question: If the aircraft radio fails in flight under VFR while operating into a tower controlled airport, what conditions must be met before a landing may be made at that airport?
questions.answer: a. Weather conditions must be at or above basic VFR weather minimums; b. Visual contact with the tower is maintained; and c. A clearance to land is received.
key_reference_points: Weather must be at or above basic VFR minimums
Must maintain visual contact with the control tower
Must receive clearance to land via light gun signals
Acknowledge light signals: day = rock wings, night = flash landing/nav lights
Referenced in 14 CFR 91.126, 91.127, 91.129

questions.question: What procedures should be used when attempting communications with a tower when the aircraft transmitter or receiver or both are inoperative? (AIM 4-2-13) Arriving Aircraft Receiver Inoperative:
questions.answer: a. Remain outside or above Class D surface area. b. Determine direction and flow of traffic. c. Advise tower of aircraft type, position, altitude, and intention to land. Request to be controlled by light signals. d. At 3 to 5 miles, advise tower of position and join traffic pattern. e. Watch tower for light gun signals. Arriving Aircraft Transmitter Inoperative: a. Remain outside or above Class D surface area. b. Determine direction and flow of traffic. c. Monitor frequency for landing or traffic information. d. Join the traffic pattern and watch for light gun signals. e. Daytime, acknowledge by rocking wings. Nighttime, acknowledge by flashing landing light or navigation lights. Arriving Aircraft Transmitter and Receiver Inoperative: a. Remain outside or above Class D surface area. b. Determine direction and flow of traffic. c. Join the traffic pattern and watch for light gun signals. d. Acknowledge light signals as noted above.
key_reference_points: Remain outside or above Class D surface area until established
Determine traffic flow direction before entering pattern
Receiver inoperative: advise tower of position and request light signals
Transmitter inoperative: monitor frequency and watch for light signals
Daytime acknowledgment: rock wings
Nighttime acknowledgment: flash landing light or navigation lights

questions.question: What general rules apply concerning traffic pattern operations at non-tower airports within Class E or G airspace? (14 CFR 91.126, 91.127) Each person operating an aircraft to or from an airport without an operating control tower shall:
questions.answer: a. in the case of an airplane approaching to land, make all turns of that airplane to the left unless the airport displays approved light signals or visual markings indicating that turns should be made to the right, in which case the pilot shall make all turns to the right. b. in the case of an aircraft departing an airport, comply with any traffic patterns established for that airport in Part 93.
key_reference_points: Standard traffic pattern turns are LEFT unless otherwise indicated
Right turns required only when airport displays approved light signals or visual markings
Must comply with any traffic patterns established in Part 93
Applies to non-towered airports in Class E or G airspace
Referenced in 14 CFR 91.126 and 91.127

questions.question: When operating in Class D airspace, what procedure should be used when approaching to land on a runway with a Visual Approach Slope Indicator? (14 CFR 91.129) Aircraft approaching to land on a runway served by a Visual Approach Slope Indicator shall maintain an altitude at or above the glide slope until a lower altitude is necessary for a safe landing.
key_reference_points: Must maintain at or above VASI glide slope when approaching runway served by VASI
Lower altitude only permitted when necessary for safe landing
Applies to all aircraft operating in Class D airspace
Reference: 14 CFR 91.129

questions.question: What is the fuel requirement for VFR flight at night? (14 CFR 91.151) No person may begin a flight in an airplane under VFR conditions unless (considering wind and forecast weather conditions) there is enough fuel to fly to the first point of intended landing and, assuming normal cruising speed, at night, to fly after that for at least 45 minutes.
key_reference_points: VFR night fuel requirement: 45 minutes reserve after reaching first point of intended landing
Reserve calculation based on normal cruising speed
Must consider wind and forecast weather conditions in fuel planning
Reference: 14 CFR 91.151

questions.question: What is the fuel requirement for VFR flight during the day? (14 CFR 91.151) During the day, you must be able to fly to the first point of intended landing, and assuming normal cruising speed, to fly after that for at least 30 minutes.
key_reference_points: VFR day fuel requirement: 30 minutes reserve after reaching first point of intended landing
Reserve calculation based on normal cruising speed
Day reserve is 15 minutes less than night reserve (30 vs 45 minutes)
Reference: 14 CFR 91.151

questions.question: When operating an aircraft under VFR in level cruising flight at an altitude of more than 3,000 feet above the surface, what rules apply concerning specific altitudes flown? (14 CFR 91.159) When operating above 3,000 feet AGL but less than 18,000 feet MSL on a magnetic course of 0° to 179°, fly at an odd-thousand-foot MSL altitude plus 500 feet. When on a magnetic course of 180° to 359°, fly at an even- thousand-foot MSL altitude plus 500 feet.
key_reference_points: Applies above 3,000 feet AGL but below 18,000 feet MSL
Magnetic course 0°-179°: odd thousand MSL + 500 feet (e.g., 3,500, 5,500, 7,500)
Magnetic course 180°-359°: even thousand MSL + 500 feet (e.g., 4,500, 6,500, 8,500)
Remember: Odd + 500 for East, Even + 500 for West
Based on MAGNETIC course, not heading or true course

questions.question: What is an “ELT”? (AIM 6-2-4) Emergency Locator Transmitter—A radio transmitter attached to the aircraft structure which operates from its own power source on 121.5, 243.0 MHz, and the newer 406 MHz. It aids in locating downed aircraft by radiating a downward-sweeping audio tone, 2 – 4 times a second. It is designed to function without human action after an accident. It can be operationally tested during the first 5 minutes after any hour. (Note: Digital 406 MHz ELTs should only be tested per the manufacturer’s instructions.)
key_reference_points: ELT transmits on 121.5 MHz, 243.0 MHz, and 406 MHz
Activates automatically upon impact without human action
Transmits downward-sweeping audio tone 2-4 times per second
Operational testing allowed only during first 5 minutes after any hour
406 MHz digital ELTs should only be tested per manufacturer instructions
Operates from its own independent power source

questions.question: Is an emergency locator transmitter required on all aircraft? (14 CFR 91.207) No person may operate a U.S. registered civil airplane unless there is attached to the airplane an automatic-type emergency locator transmitter that is in operable condition. Several exceptions exist, including the following:
questions.answer: a. Aircraft engaged in training operations conducted entirely within a 50- nautical-mile radius of the airport from which such local flight operations began. b. Aircraft engaged in design and testing. c. New aircraft engaged in manufacture, preparation and delivery. d. Aircraft engaged in agricultural operations.
key_reference_points: ELT required on U.S. registered civil aircraft and must be operable
Exception: Training flights within 50 NM radius of departure airport
Exception: Aircraft engaged in design and testing
Exception: New aircraft in manufacture, preparation, and delivery
Exception: Agricultural operations
Must be automatic-type ELT

questions.question: When must the batteries in an emergency locator transmitter be replaced or recharged, if rechargeable? (14 CFR 91.207) Batteries used in ELTs must be replaced (or recharged, if the batteries are rechargeable):
questions.answer: a. When the transmitter has been in use for more than 1 cumulative hour; or b. When 50 percent of their useful life (or, rechargeable batteries, 50 percent of their useful life of charge), has expired.
key_reference_points: Replace/recharge when transmitter used for more than 1 cumulative hour
Replace/recharge when 50% of useful battery life has expired
For rechargeable batteries, 50% of useful life of charge is the threshold
Battery replacement date must be marked on the outside of the transmitter
Reference: 14 CFR 91.207
questions.note: The new expiration date for replacing (or recharging) the battery must be legibly marked on the outside of the transmitter and entered in the aircraft maintenance record. This date indicates 50% of the battery’s useful life.

questions.question: What are the regulations concerning use of supplemental oxygen on board an aircraft?
questions.answer: a. At cabin pressure altitudes above 12,500 feet MSL up to and including 14,000 feet MSL: for that part of the flight at those altitudes that is more than 30 minutes, the required minimum flight crew must be provided with and use supplemental oxygen. b. At cabin pressure altitudes above 14,000 feet MSL: for the entire flight time at those altitudes, the required flight crew is provided with and uses supplemental oxygen. c. At cabin pressure altitudes above 15,000 feet MSL: each occupant is provided with supplemental oxygen.
key_reference_points: 12,500–14,000 ft MSL: crew must use O2 after 30 minutes at those altitudes
Above 14,000 ft MSL: crew must use O2 for entire flight time at those altitudes
Above 15,000 ft MSL: all occupants must be provided supplemental oxygen
Rules based on cabin pressure altitude, not indicated altitude
Reference: 14 CFR 91.211

questions.question: According to regulations, where is aerobatic flight of an aircraft not permitted? (14 CFR 91.303) No person may operate an aircraft in aerobatic flight:
questions.answer: a. Over any congested area of a city, town, or settlement; b. Over an open air assembly of persons; c. Within the lateral boundaries of the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E airspace designated for an airport; d. Within 4 nautical miles of the center line of a Federal airway; e. Below an altitude of 1,500 feet above the surface; or f. When flight visibility is less than 3 statute miles.
key_reference_points: No aerobatics over congested areas or open air assemblies of persons
No aerobatics within Class B, C, D, or E surface areas designated for airports
No aerobatics within 4 NM of Federal airway centerline
No aerobatics below 1,500 feet AGL
No aerobatics when flight visibility is less than 3 statute miles
Reference: 14 CFR 91.303

questions.question: Define aerobatic flight. (14 CFR 91.303) For the purposes of this section, aerobatic flight means an intentional maneuver involving an abrupt change in an aircraft’s attitude, an abnormal attitude, or abnormal acceleration, not necessary for normal flight.
key_reference_points: Aerobatic flight = intentional maneuver involving abrupt attitude change
Includes abnormal attitude or abnormal acceleration
Maneuver must NOT be necessary for normal flight
Reference: 14 CFR 91.303

questions.question: When are parachutes required on board an aircraft?
questions.answer: a. Unless each occupant of the aircraft is wearing an approved parachute, no pilot of a civil aircraft carrying any person (other than a crewmember) may execute any intentional maneuver that exceeds: • a bank angle of 60° relative to the horizon; or • a nose-up or nose-down attitude of 30° relative to the horizon. b. The above regulation does not apply to: • flight tests for pilot certification or rating; or • spins and other flight maneuvers required by the regulations for any certificate or rating when given by a CFI or ATP instructing in accordance with 14 CFR §61.67.
key_reference_points: Parachutes required if bank exceeds 60° relative to horizon
Parachutes required if pitch exceeds 30° nose-up or nose-down
Applies when carrying any person other than crewmember
Exception: Flight tests for pilot certification or rating
Exception: Spins/maneuvers required for certificate when given by CFI or ATP per 14 CFR 61.67
Reference: 14 CFR 91.307

questions.question: What is Class A airspace? (AIM 3-2-2) Generally, that airspace from 18,000 feet MSL up to and including FL600, including that airspace overlying the waters within 12 nautical miles of the coast of the 48 contiguous states and Alaska; and designated international airspace beyond 12 nautical miles of the coast of the 48 contiguous states and Alaska within areas of domestic radio navigational signal or ATC radar coverage, and within which domestic procedures are applied.
key_reference_points: Class A airspace: 18,000 feet MSL up to and including FL600
Extends 12 NM off coast of 48 contiguous states and Alaska
All operations must be conducted under IFR
Pilot must be instrument rated and aircraft instrument equipped
ATC clearance required at all times

questions.question: Can a flight under VFR be conducted within Class A airspace? (14 CFR 91.135) No, unless otherwise authorized by ATC, each person operating an aircraft in Class A airspace must operate that aircraft under instrument flight rules (IFR).
key_reference_points: Class A airspace requires IFR flight only - VFR is prohibited
ATC authorization is required for any exception to IFR requirement
Class A airspace extends from 18,000 feet MSL to FL600
