14 CFR 91.135 governs Class A airspace operations

questions.question: What is the minimum pilot certification for operations conducted within Class A airspace? (14 CFR 91.135) The pilot must be at least a private pilot with an instrument rating.
key_reference_points: Minimum certification: Private Pilot Certificate with Instrument Rating
Student pilots cannot operate in Class A airspace
Sport and recreational pilots cannot operate in Class A airspace
Instrument rating required because all Class A operations must be IFR

questions.question: What minimum equipment is required for flight operations within Class A airspace?
questions.answer: a. A two-way radio capable of communicating with ATC on the frequency assigned. b. A Mode C altitude encoding transponder. c. Equipped with instruments and equipment required for IFR operations.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio capable of communicating on ATC-assigned frequencies
Mode C transponder with altitude encoding capability required
All IFR-required instruments and equipment must be installed and operational
IFR equipment includes attitude indicator, heading indicator, altimeter, turn coordinator, etc.
ADS-B Out equipment also required in Class A airspace

questions.question: How is Class A airspace depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-2-2) Class A airspace is not specifically charted.
key_reference_points: Class A airspace is NOT depicted on VFR sectional charts
Class A airspace exists from 18,000 feet MSL to FL600
Pilots must reference the AIM or IFR charts for Class A airspace information
VFR flight is not permitted in Class A airspace

questions.question: What is the definition of Class B airspace? (AIM 3-2-3) Generally, that airspace from the surface to 10,000 feet MSL surrounding the nation’s busiest airports in terms of IFR operations or passenger enplanements. The configuration of each Class B airspace area is individually tailored and consists of a surface area and two or more layers (some Class B airspace areas resemble upside down wedding cakes), and is designated to contain all published instrument procedures once an aircraft enters the airspace.
key_reference_points: Class B airspace extends from the surface to 10,000 feet MSL
Surrounds the nation's busiest airports (IFR operations or passenger volume)
Configuration resembles an upside-down wedding cake with multiple layers
Each Class B airspace is individually tailored to contain all published instrument procedures
Depicted on sectional charts with solid blue lines

questions.question: What minimum pilot certification is required to operate an aircraft within Class B airspace? (14 CFR 91.131) No person may take off or land a civil aircraft at an airport within a Class B airspace area or operate a civil aircraft within a Class B airspace area unless:
questions.answer: a. The pilot-in-command holds at least a private pilot certificate; b. The pilot-in-command holds a recreational pilot certificate and has met the requirements of 14 CFR §61.101; or for a student pilot seeking a recreational pilot certificate met the requirements of 14 CFR §61.94. c. The pilot-in-command holds a sport pilot certificate and has met the requirements of 14 CFR §61.325; or the requirements for a student pilot seeking a recreational pilot certificate in 14 CFR §61.94. d. The aircraft is operated by a student pilot who has met the requirements of 14 CFR §61.94 or §61.95 of this chapter, as applicable. Certain Class B airspace areas do not allow pilot operations to be conducted to or from the primary airport, unless the pilot-in-command holds at least a private pilot certificate (example: Dallas/Fort Worth International).
key_reference_points: Private pilot certificate or higher required for most Class B operations
Student pilots need specific endorsements per 14 CFR 61.94 or 61.95
Recreational pilots must meet requirements of 14 CFR 61.101
Sport pilots must meet requirements of 14 CFR 61.325 or 61.94
Some primary airports (e.g., DFW) require at least a private pilot certificate—no student pilot operations allowed
Reference regulation: 14 CFR 91.131

questions.question: What is the minimum equipment required for operations of an aircraft within Class B airspace?
questions.answer: a. An operable two-way radio capable of communications with ATC on the appropriate frequencies for that area. b. A Mode C altitude encoding transponder. c. If IFR, an operable VOR or TACAN receiver or an operable and suitable RNAV system.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio capable of communicating with ATC on appropriate frequencies
Mode C transponder (altitude encoding) is required
For IFR operations: operable VOR, TACAN, or suitable RNAV system required
Reference: 14 CFR 91.131
Memory aid: 'Radio, Transponder, Navigation (if IFR)'

questions.question: Before operating an aircraft into Class B airspace, what basic requirement must be met? (14 CFR 91.131) Arriving aircraft must obtain an ATC clearance from the ATC facility having jurisdiction for that area prior to operating an aircraft in that area.
key_reference_points: ATC clearance MUST be obtained BEFORE entering Class B airspace
Clearance must come from the ATC facility with jurisdiction over that specific Class B
Hearing your callsign alone is NOT clearance - must hear 'cleared into Class Bravo'
Reference: 14 CFR 91.131
No clearance = no entry, regardless of weather conditions

questions.question: What minimum weather conditions are required when conducting VFR flight operations within Class B airspace? (14 CFR 91.155) VFR flight operations must be conducted clear of clouds with at least 3 statute miles flight visibility.
key_reference_points: Minimum visibility: 3 statute miles flight visibility
Cloud clearance: Clear of clouds (no specific distance required)
Class B is the only airspace where VFR requires only 'clear of clouds'
Reference: 14 CFR 91.155
Memory aid: '3 miles, clear of clouds' for Class B VFR

questions.question: How is Class B airspace depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-2-3) Class B airspace is charted on Sectional Charts, IFR En Route Low Altitude, and Terminal Area Charts. A solid shaded blue line depicts the lateral limits of Class B airspace. Numbers indicate the base and top, i.e. 100/25, 100/SFC.
key_reference_points: Class B depicted on Sectional Charts, IFR En Route Low Altitude, and Terminal Area Charts
Solid shaded BLUE line shows lateral limits of Class B airspace
Numbers show ceiling/floor (e.g., 100/25 means 10,000 MSL ceiling, 2,500 MSL floor)
SFC indicates the floor extends to the surface (e.g., 100/SFC)

questions.question: What basic ATC services are provided to all aircraft operating within Class B airspace? (AIM 3-2-3) VFR pilots will be provided sequencing and separation from other aircraft while operating within Class B airspace.
key_reference_points: VFR pilots receive sequencing services in Class B airspace
VFR pilots receive separation from other aircraft in Class B airspace
ATC clearance is required to enter Class B airspace
These services are provided to ALL aircraft (VFR and IFR) in Class B

questions.question: It becomes apparent that wake turbulence may be encountered while ATC is providing sequencing and separation services in Class B airspace. Whose responsibility is it to avoid this turbulence? (AIM 3-2-3) The pilot-in-command is responsible. The services provided by ATC do not relieve pilots of their responsibilities to see and avoid other traffic operating in basic VFR weather conditions, to adjust their operations and flight path as necessary to preclude serious wake turbulence encounters, to maintain appropriate terrain and obstruction clearance, or to remain in weather conditions equal to or better than the minimums required by 14 CFR §91.155.
key_reference_points: Pilot-in-command is ALWAYS responsible for wake turbulence avoidance
ATC sequencing/separation does NOT relieve pilot of see-and-avoid responsibility
Pilot must maintain appropriate terrain and obstruction clearance
Pilot must remain in weather conditions meeting or exceeding 14 CFR §91.155 VFR minimums
Pilot must adjust flight path to preclude serious wake turbulence encounters

questions.question: What is the maximum speed allowed when operating inside Class B airspace, under 10,000 feet and within a Class D surface area? (14 CFR 91.117) Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft at or below 2,500 feet above the surface within 4 nautical miles of the primary airport of a Class C or Class D airspace area at an indicated airspeed of more than 200 knots. This restriction does not apply to operations conducted within a Class B airspace area. Such operations shall comply with the “below 10,000 feet MSL” restriction: “No person shall operate an aircraft below 10,000 feet MSL, at an indicated airspeed of more than 250 knots.”
key_reference_points: Class B airspace: max 250 knots below 10,000 feet MSL
Class D surface area within 4 NM of primary airport: max 200 knots at or below 2,500 AGL
The 200-knot restriction near Class C/D airports does NOT apply inside Class B airspace
Class B operations must still comply with the 250-knot limit below 10,000 feet MSL
Speed limits can be modified if authorized or required by ATC

questions.question: When operating beneath the lateral limits of Class B airspace, or in a VFR corridor designated through Class B airspace, what maximum speed is authorized? (14 CFR 91.117) No person may operate an aircraft in the airspace underlying a Class B airspace area designated for an airport or in a VFR corridor designated through such a Class B airspace area, at an indicated airspeed of more than 200 knots (230 MPH).
key_reference_points: Max 200 knots (230 MPH) when operating beneath Class B airspace
Max 200 knots (230 MPH) in VFR corridors through Class B airspace
This applies to the airspace UNDERLYING Class B, not inside Class B
Reference: 14 CFR 91.117

questions.question: What is Class C airspace? (AIM 3-2-4) Generally, that airspace from the surface to 4,000 feet above the airport elevation (charted in MSL) surrounding those airports that have an operational control tower, are serviced by a radar approach control, and that have a certain number of IFR operations or passenger enplanements.
key_reference_points: Class C airspace extends from surface to 4,000 feet above airport elevation
Altitudes are charted in MSL (Mean Sea Level)
Requires operational control tower at the airport
Must be serviced by radar approach control
Typically has two circles: inner 5 NM surface area, outer 10 NM shelf starting at 1,200 AGL
Designated based on IFR operations or passenger enplanement numbers

questions.question: What are the basic dimensions of Class C airspace? (AIM 3-2-4) Although the configuration of each Class C airspace area is individually tailored, the airspace usually consists of a 5 NM radius core surface area that extends from the surface up to 4,000 feet above the airport elevation, and a 10 NM radius shelf area that extends from 1,200 feet to 4,000 feet above the airport elevation. The outer area radius will be 20 NM, with some variations based on site specific requirements. The outer area extends outward from the primary airport and extends from the lower limits of radar/radio coverage up to the ceiling of the approach controls airspace.
key_reference_points: Core surface area: 5 NM radius from surface to 4,000 feet AGL
Shelf area: 10 NM radius from 1,200 feet AGL to 4,000 feet AGL
Outer area: 20 NM radius (varies by site)
Class C resembles an upside-down wedding cake shape
Outer area extends from lower limits of radar/radio coverage to approach control ceiling

questions.question: What minimum pilot certification is required to operate an aircraft within Class C airspace? (AIM 3-2-4) A student pilot certificate.
key_reference_points: Minimum certification required: Student Pilot Certificate
Student pilots may operate in Class C with appropriate training and endorsements
No special Class C endorsement required (unlike Class B)

questions.question: What minimum equipment is required to operate an aircraft within Class C airspace? (14 CFR 91.130, 91.215) Unless otherwise authorized by the ATC having jurisdiction over the Class C airspace area, no person may operate an aircraft within a Class C airspace area designated for an airport unless that aircraft is equipped with the following:
questions.answer: a. A two-way radio. b. Automatic pressure altitude reporting equipment with Mode C capability.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio capable of communicating with ATC
Mode C transponder with automatic pressure altitude reporting capability
Equipment requirements found in 14 CFR 91.130 and 91.215
ATC may authorize deviations from equipment requirements

questions.question: When operating an aircraft through Class C airspace or to an airport within Class C airspace, what basic requirement must be met? (14 CFR 91.130) Each person must establish two-way radio communications with the ATC facilities providing air traffic services prior to entering that airspace and thereafter maintain those communications while within that airspace.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio communications must be established BEFORE entering Class C airspace
Communications must be maintained while operating within Class C airspace
Requirement applies to all operations through Class C or to airports within Class C
Reference: 14 CFR 91.130

questions.question: Two-way radio communications must be established prior to entering Class C airspace. Define what is meant by “established” in this context. (AIM 3-2-4) If a controller responds to a radio call with, “(aircraft call sign) standby,” radio communications have been established. It is important to understand that if the controller responds to the initial radio call without using the aircraft identification, radio communications have not been established and the pilot may not enter the Class C airspace.
key_reference_points: Communications are ESTABLISHED when controller responds using YOUR aircraft callsign
If controller says '(your callsign) standby' - you ARE cleared to enter
If controller responds WITHOUT using your callsign - communications NOT established, do NOT enter
Key distinction: callsign acknowledgment = established; no callsign = not established
Reference: AIM 3-2-4

questions.question: When departing a satellite airport without an operative control tower located within Class C airspace, what requirement must be met? (14 CFR 91.130) Each person must establish and maintain two-way radio communications with the ATC facilities having jurisdiction over the Class C airspace area as soon as practicable after departing.
key_reference_points: Applies to satellite airports without operative control towers within Class C
Must establish two-way radio communications as soon as PRACTICABLE after departing
Contact ATC facility having jurisdiction over that Class C airspace
Requirement is 'as soon as practicable' not 'prior to' since departing from within
Reference: 14 CFR 91.130

questions.question: What minimum weather conditions are required when conducting VFR flight operations within Class C airspace? (14 CFR 91.155) VFR flight operations within Class C airspace require 3 statute miles flight visibility and cloud clearances of at least 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above and 2,000 feet horizontal to clouds.
key_reference_points: Class C airspace requires 3 statute miles flight visibility
Cloud clearance: 500 feet below clouds
Cloud clearance: 1,000 feet above clouds
Cloud clearance: 2,000 feet horizontal from clouds
Reference: 14 CFR 91.155

questions.question: How is Class C airspace depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-2- 4) A solid magenta line is used to depict Class C airspace. Class C airspace is charted on Sectional Charts, IFR En Route Low Altitude, and Terminal Area Charts where appropriate.
key_reference_points: Class C airspace depicted by solid MAGENTA line
Found on Sectional Charts, IFR En Route Low Altitude, and Terminal Area Charts
Typically shown as two circles: inner 5 NM surface area and outer 10 NM shelf
Reference: AIM 3-2-4

questions.question: What type of Air Traffic Control services are provided when operating within Class C airspace? (AIM 3-2-4) When two-way radio communications and radar contact are established, all VFR aircraft are:
questions.answer: a. Sequenced to the primary airport. b. Provided Class C services within the Class C airspace and the outer area. c. Provided basic radar services beyond the outer area on a workload permitting basis. This can be terminated by the controller if workload dictates.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio communication AND radar contact must be established before entering
VFR aircraft are sequenced to the primary airport
Class C services provided within Class C airspace and outer area
Basic radar services beyond outer area on workload-permitting basis
Controller may terminate services beyond outer area if workload requires

questions.question: Describe the various types of terminal radar services available for VFR aircraft. (AIM 4-1-18) Basic radar service—Safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring (on a workload-permitting basis) and sequencing at locations where procedures have been established for this purpose and/or when covered by a letter of agreement. TRSA service—radar sequencing and separation service for VFR aircraft in a TRSA. Class C service—This service provides, in addition to basic radar service, approved separation between IFR and VFR aircraft, and sequencing of VFR arrivals to the primary airport. Class B service—Provides, in addition to basic radar service, approved separation of aircraft based on IFR, VFR, and/or weight, and sequencing of VFR arrivals to the primary airport(s).
key_reference_points: Basic radar service: safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited vectoring (workload permitting)
TRSA service: radar sequencing and separation for VFR aircraft within TRSA
Class C service: basic radar plus IFR/VFR separation and VFR arrival sequencing
Class B service: basic radar plus separation based on IFR/VFR/weight and VFR sequencing
All terminal radar services for VFR are provided on workload-permitting basis except where required

questions.question: Where is Mode C altitude encoding transponder equipment required? (AIM 4-1-20)
questions.answer: a. At or above 10,000 feet MSL over the 48 contiguous states or the District of Columbia, excluding that airspace below 2,500 feet AGL. b. Within 30 miles of a Class B airspace primary airport, below 10,000 feet MSL c. Within and above all Class C airspace, up to 10,000 feet MSL; d. Within 10 miles of certain designated airports, excluding that airspace which is both outside the Class D surface area and below 1,200 feet AGL. e. All aircraft flying into, within, or across the contiguous U.S. ADIZ.
key_reference_points: At or above 10,000 feet MSL over contiguous US (except below 2,500 AGL)
Within 30 NM of Class B primary airport, below 10,000 feet MSL (Mode C veil)
Within and above Class C airspace up to 10,000 feet MSL
Within 10 NM of designated airports (outside Class D and below 1,200 AGL exempt)
All aircraft in the contiguous US ADIZ
questions.note: Civil and military transponders should be turned to the “on” or normal altitude reporting position prior to moving on the airport surface to ensure the aircraft is visible to ATC surveillance systems.

questions.question: What is the maximum speed an aircraft may be operated within Class C airspace? (AIM 3-2-4) Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft at or below 2,500 feet above the surface within 4 nautical miles of the primary airport of a Class C airspace area at an indicated speed of more than 200 knots (230 MPH).
key_reference_points: Maximum 200 knots indicated airspeed within Class C airspace
Speed limit applies at or below 2,500 feet AGL
Speed limit applies within 4 NM of the Class C primary airport
Speed may exceed 200 knots only if authorized or required by ATC

questions.question: What is Class D airspace? (AIM 3-2-5) Generally, Class D airspace extends upward from the surface to 2,500 feet above the airport elevation (charted in MSL) surrounding those airports that have an operational control tower. The configuration of each Class D airspace area is individually tailored and when instrument procedures are published, the airspace will normally be designed to contain those procedures.
key_reference_points: Class D airspace extends from surface to 2,500 feet AGL (charted in MSL)
Surrounds airports with an operational control tower
Configuration is individually tailored for each airport
Designed to contain instrument procedures when published
Reference: AIM 3-2-5

questions.question: When operating an aircraft through Class D airspace or to an airport within Class D airspace, what requirement must be met? (14 CFR 91.129) Each person must establish two-way radio communications with the ATC facilities providing air traffic services prior to entering that airspace and thereafter maintain those communications while within that airspace.
key_reference_points: Must establish two-way radio communications BEFORE entering Class D airspace
Must maintain communications while operating within Class D airspace
Communications must be with ATC facility providing air traffic services
Requirement applies to transiting through OR landing at airport within Class D
Reference: 14 CFR 91.129

questions.question: When departing a satellite airport without an operative control tower located within Class D airspace, what requirement must be met? (14 CFR 91.129) Each person must establish and maintain two-way radio communications with the ATC facility having jurisdiction over the Class D airspace area as soon as practicable after departing.
key_reference_points: Applies to satellite airports without operative control towers within Class D
Must establish two-way radio communications as soon as practicable after departing
Contact the ATC facility having jurisdiction over that Class D airspace
Must maintain communications once established
Reference: 14 CFR 91.129

questions.question: Is an ATC clearance required if flight operations are conducted through a Class E surface area arrival extension? (AIM 3-2-5, 3-2-6) Class E airspace may be designated as extensions to Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas. Class E airspace extensions begin at the surface and extend up to the overlying controlled airspace. The extensions provide controlled airspace to contain standard instrument approach procedures without imposing a communications requirement on pilots operating under VFR. Surface area arrival extensions become part of the surface area and are in effect during the same times as the surface area.
key_reference_points: Class E surface area extensions do NOT require ATC clearance for VFR operations
Extensions provide controlled airspace for instrument approaches without VFR communication requirements
Extensions begin at the surface and extend up to overlying controlled airspace
Surface area arrival extensions are active during the same times as the associated surface area
Class E extensions can be associated with Class B, C, D, or E surface areas

questions.question: What minimum weather conditions are required when conducting VFR flight operations within Class D airspace? (14 CFR 91.155) VFR flight operations within Class D airspace require 3 statute miles flight visibility and cloud clearances of at least 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above and 2,000 feet horizontal to clouds.
key_reference_points: Class D requires 3 statute miles flight visibility
Cloud clearance: 500 feet below clouds
Cloud clearance: 1,000 feet above clouds
Cloud clearance: 2,000 feet horizontal from clouds
Reference regulation: 14 CFR 91.155

questions.question: How is Class D airspace depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-2- 5) Class D airspace areas are depicted on Sectional and Terminal charts with blue segmented lines, and on IFR Enroute Lows with a boxed [D].
key_reference_points: Sectional charts: blue segmented (dashed) lines
Terminal Area Charts: blue segmented lines
IFR Enroute Low charts: boxed [D] symbol
Blue dashed lines distinguish Class D from Class B/C solid blue lines

questions.question: What type of Air Traffic Control services are provided when operating within Class D airspace? (AIM 3-2-5, 5-5-8, and 5-5-10) No separation services are provided to VFR aircraft. When meteorological conditions permit, regardless of the type of flight plan or whether or not under the control of a radar facility, the pilot is responsible to see and avoid other traffic, terrain or obstacles. A controller, on a workload permitting basis, will provide radar traffic information, safety alerts and traffic information for sequencing purposes.
key_reference_points: NO separation services provided to VFR aircraft in Class D
Pilot is responsible for see-and-avoid regardless of flight plan or radar contact
ATC provides radar traffic information on workload-permitting basis only
Safety alerts are provided on workload-permitting basis
Traffic sequencing information may be provided but is not guaranteed

questions.question: What is the maximum speed an aircraft may be operated within Class D airspace? (AIM 3-2-5) Unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft at or below 2,500 feet above the surface within 4 nautical miles of the primary airport of a Class D airspace area at an indicated airspeed of more than 200 knots (230 MPH).
key_reference_points: Maximum speed: 200 knots (230 MPH) indicated airspeed
Speed limit applies at or below 2,500 feet AGL
Speed limit applies within 4 nautical miles of the primary Class D airport
Speed may exceed 200 knots only if authorized or required by ATC
Reference: AIM 3-2-5

questions.question: When a control tower, located at an airport within Class D airspace, ceases operation for the day, what happens to the lower limit of the controlled airspace? (AIM 3-2-5) During the hours the tower is not in operation, Class E surface area rules, or a combination of Class E rules down to 700 feet AGL and Class G rules to the surface, will become applicable. Check the Chart Supplement U.S. for specifics.
key_reference_points: When tower closes, Class D airspace reverts to Class E or Class E/G combination
Class E surface area rules may apply when tower is closed
Some airports revert to Class E down to 700 feet AGL with Class G below
Always check Chart Supplement U.S. for specific airport procedures
Hours of tower operation determine when Class D is active

questions.question: Will all airports with an operating control tower always have Class D airspace surrounding them? (AIM 4-3-2) No; some airports do not have the required weather reporting capability necessary for surface based controlled airspace. The controlled airspace over these airports normally begins at 700 feet or 1,200 feet AGL and can be determined from visual aeronautical charts.
key_reference_points: Not all airports with operating control towers have Class D airspace
Class D requires weather reporting capability for surface-based controlled airspace
Airports lacking weather reporting have controlled airspace beginning at 700 ft or 1,200 ft AGL instead
Check visual aeronautical charts to determine actual airspace floor altitude

questions.question: What is the definition of Class E (controlled) airspace? (AIM Glossary) Controlled airspace is airspace of defined dimensions within which air traffic control service is provided to IFR flights and to VFR flights in accordance with the airspace classification. Controlled airspace is a generic term that covers Class A, Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E airspace.
key_reference_points: Class E is controlled airspace with defined dimensions
ATC service is provided to IFR flights in Class E
VFR flights receive ATC service in accordance with airspace classification
Controlled airspace includes Class A, B, C, D, and E
Class E is the most common type of controlled airspace

questions.question: State several examples of Class E airspace. (AIM 3-2-6)
questions.answer: a. Surface area designated for an airport where a control tower is not in operation—Class E surface areas extend upward from the surface to a designated altitude, or to the adjacent or overlying controlled airspace and are configured to contain all instrument procedures. b. Extension to a surface area—Class E airspace may be designated as extensions to Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas. Class E airspace extensions begin at the surface and extend up to the overlying controlled airspace. The extensions provide controlled airspace to contain standard instrument approach procedures without imposing a communications requirement on pilots operating under VFR. c. Airspace used for transition—Class E airspace areas may be designated for transitioning aircraft to/from the terminal or enroute environment. They extend upward from either 700 feet AGL or 1,200 feet AGL and are designated for airports with an approved instrument procedure. The 700- foot/1,200-foot AGL Class E airspace transition areas remain in effect continuously, regardless of airport operating hours or surface area status. d. Enroute domestic areas—Class E airspace areas that extend upward from a specified altitude and provide controlled airspace in those areas where there is a requirement to provide IFR enroute ATC services but the Federal airway system is inadequate. e. Federal airways and low-altitude RNAV routes—Federal airways and low-altitude RNAV routes are Class E airspace areas and, unless otherwise specified, extend upward from 1,200 feet AGL to, but not including 18,000 feet MSL. f. Offshore airspace areas—Class E airspace areas that extend upward from a specified altitude to, but not including 18,000 feet MSL. These areas provide controlled airspace beyond 12 miles from the coast of the U.S. in those areas where there is a requirement to provide IFR enroute ATC services, and within which the U.S. is applying domestic procedures. g. Unless designated at a lower altitude—Class E airspace in the U.S. consists of the airspace extending upward from 14,500 feet MSL to, but not including 18,000 feet MSL overlying the 48 contiguous states, the District of Columbia and Alaska, including the waters within 12 NM from the coast of the 48 contiguous states and Alaska. h. The airspace above FL 600 is Class E airspace.
key_reference_points: Surface area Class E: designated for airports without operating control tower, extends from surface upward
Extensions to surface areas: Class E extensions begin at surface for instrument approaches without VFR communication requirements
Transition areas: begin at 700 ft AGL (magenta vignette) or 1,200 ft AGL (blue vignette) for airports with instrument procedures
Federal airways: Class E from 1,200 ft AGL to but not including 18,000 ft MSL
General Class E floor: 14,500 ft MSL to but not including 18,000 ft MSL over contiguous 48 states and Alaska
Above FL 600 is Class E airspace

questions.question: What are the operating rules and pilot/equipment requirements to operate within Class E airspace? (AIM 3-2-6)
questions.answer: a. Minimum pilot certification—student pilot certificate. b. No specific equipment requirements in Class E airspace. c. No specific requirements for arrival or through flight in Class E airspace.
key_reference_points: Minimum pilot certificate required: Student Pilot Certificate
No specific equipment requirements for Class E airspace
No specific clearance or communication requirements for VFR flight
No ATC authorization required for arrival or through flight
Standard VFR weather minimums apply (3 SM visibility, cloud clearances vary by altitude)

questions.question: When a Class C or Class D surface area is not in effect continuously (for example, where a control tower only operates part-time), what will happen to the surface area airspace when the tower closes? (AIM 3-2-6) The surface area airspace will change to either a Class E surface area or Class G airspace. In such cases, the “Airspace” entry for the airport in the Chart Supplement U.S. will state “other times Class E” or “other times Class G.” When a part-time surface area changes to Class E airspace, the Class E arrival extensions will remain in effect as Class E airspace. If a part–time Class C, Class D, or Class E surface area becomes Class G airspace, the arrival extensions will change to Class G at the same time.
key_reference_points: Part-time Class C/D surface areas convert to Class E or Class G when tower closes
Chart Supplement U.S. 'Airspace' entry specifies 'other times Class E' or 'other times Class G'
When surface area becomes Class E, arrival extensions remain Class E
When surface area becomes Class G, arrival extensions also become Class G
Pilot must check Chart Supplement to determine airspace status when tower is closed

questions.question: Explain the purpose of Class E transition areas. (AIM 3-2-6) Class E transition areas extend upward from either 700 feet AGL (magenta vignette) or 1,200 feet AGL (blue vignette) and are designated for airports with an approved instrument procedure. Class E transition areas exist to help separate (via cloud clearance) arriving and departing IFR traffic from VFR aircraft operating in the vicinity.
key_reference_points: 700 feet AGL floor indicated by magenta vignette on sectional charts
1,200 feet AGL floor indicated by blue vignette on sectional charts
Designated for airports with approved instrument approach procedures
Purpose: Separate IFR traffic from VFR traffic via cloud clearance requirements
Transition areas extend upward to overlying controlled airspace (typically 18,000 feet MSL)

questions.question: Are you required to establish communications with a tower located within Class E airspace? (14 CFR 91.127) Yes; unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft to, from, through, or on an airport having an operational control tower unless two-way communications are maintained between that aircraft and the control tower. Communications must be established prior to 4 nautical miles from the airport, up to and including 2,500 feet AGL.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio communication required with tower at airports with operational control tower
Applies to operations to, from, through, or on the airport
Must establish communication prior to 4 NM from airport
Communication required up to and including 2,500 feet AGL
Reference: 14 CFR 91.127

questions.question: How is Class E airspace depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-2-6; USRGD) Class E airspace below 14,500 feet MSL is charted on Sectional, Terminal, and IFR Enroute Low Altitude charts. The lateral and vertical limits of all Class E controlled airspace up to but not including 18,000 feet are shown by narrow bands of vignette on Sectional and Terminal Area charts. Controlled airspace floors of 700 feet AGL are defined by a magenta vignette; floors other than 700 feet that abut uncontrolled airspace are defined by a blue vignette; differing floors greater than 700 feet AGL are annotated by a symbol and a number indicating the floor. If the ceiling is less than 18,000 feet MSL, the value (prefixed by the word “ceiling”) is shown along the limits of the controlled airspace.
key_reference_points: Class E below 14,500 MSL shown on Sectional, Terminal, and IFR Enroute Low Altitude charts
Magenta vignette indicates 700 feet AGL floor
Blue vignette indicates floors other than 700 feet AGL abutting uncontrolled airspace
Narrow bands of vignette show lateral and vertical limits up to but not including 18,000 feet
Ceiling values prefixed with 'ceiling' when less than 18,000 feet MSL

questions.question: How are Class E surface extension areas depicted on navigational charts? (USRGD) Class E airspace areas that serve as extensions to Class B, Class C, and Class D airspace are depicted by a magenta segmented line.
key_reference_points: Class E surface extensions depicted by magenta segmented (dashed) line
These extensions serve Class B, Class C, and Class D surface areas
Provides controlled airspace for IFR arrival/departure procedures

questions.question: What is the definition of Class G airspace? (AIM 3-3-1) Class G or uncontrolled airspace is that portion of the airspace that has not been designated as Class A, B, C, D, or E airspace. It is airspace in which air traffic control has no authority or responsibility to control air traffic; however, pilots should remember there are VFR minimums that apply to this airspace.
key_reference_points: Class G is uncontrolled airspace - ATC has no authority or responsibility to control traffic
Defined by exclusion: airspace NOT designated as Class A, B, C, D, or E
VFR weather minimums still apply in Class G airspace
Pilots are still responsible for see-and-avoid and regulatory compliance

questions.question: Are you required to establish communications with a tower located within Class G airspace? (14 CFR 91.126) Yes; unless otherwise authorized or required by ATC, no person may operate an aircraft to, from, through, or on an airport having an operational control tower unless two-way communications are maintained between that aircraft and the control tower. Communications must be established prior to 4 nautical miles from the airport, up to and including 2,500 AGL.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio communication required with any operational control tower regardless of airspace class
Must establish communication prior to 4 NM from the airport
Communication requirement extends up to and including 2,500 feet AGL
Reference: 14 CFR 91.126
Exception only if otherwise authorized or required by ATC

questions.question: What are the vertical limits of Class G airspace? (FAA-H-8083-25) Class G airspace begins at the surface and continues up to the overlying controlled (Class E) airspace, not to exceed 14,500 feet MSL.
key_reference_points: Class G airspace begins at the surface
Extends upward to the base of overlying Class E airspace
Maximum vertical limit is 14,500 feet MSL
Class G floor is always the surface; ceiling varies by location based on Class E floor

questions.question: What is the minimum cloud clearance and visibility required when conducting flight operations in a traffic pattern at night in Class G airspace below 1,200 feet AGL? (14 CFR 91.155) When the visibility is less than 3 statute miles but not less than 1 statute mile during night hours, an airplane may be operated clear of clouds if operated in an airport traffic pattern within one-half mile of the runway.
key_reference_points: Night Class G below 1,200 AGL requires 3 SM visibility and 500 below/1,000 above/2,000 horizontal cloud clearance
Exception: Within 1/2 mile of runway in traffic pattern, can operate clear of clouds with 1-3 SM visibility at night
This special provision only applies to airport traffic pattern operations
Reference regulation is 14 CFR 91.155

questions.question: What is the main difference between Class G airspace and Class A, B, C, D, and E airspace? The main difference which distinguishes Class G airspace from Class A, B, C, D, and E airspace is the flight visibility/cloud clearance requirements necessary to operate within it.
key_reference_points: Class G is uncontrolled airspace; Classes A, B, C, D, E are controlled airspace
Class G has lower visibility and cloud clearance requirements than controlled airspace
Class G daytime below 1,200 AGL only requires 1 SM visibility and clear of clouds
Controlled airspace (C, D, E below 10,000) requires 3 SM visibility minimum

questions.question: What minimum flight visibility and clearance from clouds are required for VFR flight in the following situations? (14 CFR 91.155) Class C, D, or E Airspace Less than 10,000 feet MSL: Visibility: 3 statute miles. Cloud clearance: 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 2,000 feet horizontal. At or above 10,000 feet MSL: Visibility: 5 statute miles. Cloud clearance: 1,000 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 1 statute mile horizontal. Class G Airspace 1,200 feet or less above the surface (regardless of MSL altitude): Day Visibility: 1 statute mile. Cloud clearance: clear of clouds Night Visibility: 3 statute miles Cloud clearance: 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 2,000 feet horizontal. More than 1,200 feet above the surface but less than 10,000 ft. MSL: Day Visibility: 1 statute mile Cloud clearance: 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 2,000 feet horizontal. Night Visibility: 3 statute miles Cloud clearance: 500 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 2,000 feet horizontal. More than 1,200 feet above the surface and at or above 10,000 feet MSL: Visibility: 5 statute miles Cloud clearance: 1,000 feet below, 1,000 feet above, 1 statute mile horizontal.
key_reference_points: Class C/D/E below 10,000 MSL: 3 SM visibility, 500 below/1,000 above/2,000 horizontal
Class C/D/E at or above 10,000 MSL: 5 SM visibility, 1,000 below/1,000 above/1 SM horizontal
Class G at 1,200 AGL or less Day: 1 SM clear of clouds; Night: 3 SM with 500/1,000/2,000
Class G above 1,200 AGL but below 10,000 MSL: Day 1 SM, Night 3 SM, both 500/1,000/2,000
At or above 10,000 MSL (any class): 5 SM visibility, 1,000/1,000/1 SM cloud clearance
Memory aid: Higher altitude = greater visibility and cloud clearance requirements

questions.question: What are the “basic” VFR weather minimums required for operation of an aircraft into Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E airspace? (14 CFR 91.155) 1,000-foot ceiling and 3 miles visibility. Except as provided in 14 CFR §91.157 (special VFR), no person may:
questions.answer: a. Operate an aircraft beneath the ceiling under VFR within the lateral boundaries of controlled airspace designated to the surface for an airport when the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet. b. Take off or land an aircraft, or enter the traffic pattern of an airport, under VFR, within the lateral boundaries of the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E airspace designated for an airport unless ground visibility at that airport is at least 3 statute miles or, if ground visibility is not reported, unless flight visibility during landing or takeoff, or while operating in the traffic pattern is at least 3 statute miles.
key_reference_points: Basic VFR in controlled airspace requires minimum 1,000-foot ceiling
Ground visibility must be at least 3 statute miles at the airport
If ground visibility not reported, flight visibility must be at least 3 statute miles
Applies to Class B, C, D, and E airspace designated to the surface
Special VFR (14 CFR 91.157) is the only exception to these minimums
Cannot operate beneath ceiling under VFR if ceiling is less than 1,000 feet

questions.question: If VFR flight minimums cannot be maintained, can a VFR flight be made into Class B, C, D, or E airspace? (AIM 4-4-6) No, with one exception. A “Special VFR clearance” may be obtained from ATC prior to operating within a Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E surface area provided the flight can remain clear of clouds with at least one statute mile ground visibility if taking off or landing or, one statute mile flight visibility for operations within Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas.
key_reference_points: Special VFR clearance must be obtained from ATC before entering surface area
Special VFR requires clear of clouds at all times
Special VFR requires minimum 1 statute mile ground visibility for takeoff/landing
Special VFR requires minimum 1 statute mile flight visibility while operating in surface areas
Applies to Class B, C, D, and E surface areas
Pilot must request Special VFR - ATC cannot offer it

questions.question: Are Special VFR clearances always available to pilots in all classes of airspace? (AIM 4-4-6) A VFR pilot may request and be given a clearance to enter, leave, or operate within most Class D and Class E surface areas and some Class B and Class C surface areas traffic permitting and providing such flight will not delay IFR operations.
key_reference_points: Special VFR is available in most Class D and Class E surface areas
Only some Class B and Class C surface areas allow Special VFR
Special VFR granted only if it will not delay IFR operations
Traffic conditions must permit Special VFR operations
Airports prohibiting Special VFR are listed in Part 91 Appendix D and marked on charts with 'NO SVFR'
questions.note: Special VFR operations by fixed wing aircraft are prohibited in some Class B and Class C surface areas due to the volume of IFR traffic. A list of these Class B and Class C surface areas is contained in 14 CFR Part 91. They are also depicted on Sectional Aeronautical Charts.

questions.question: If it becomes apparent that a special VFR clearance will be necessary, what facility should the pilot contact in order to obtain one? (AIM 4-4-6) When a control tower is located within a Class B, Class C, or Class D surface area, requests for clearances should be made to the tower. In a Class E surface area, a clearance may be obtained from the nearest tower, FSS, or center.
key_reference_points: Contact the control tower if located within Class B, C, or D surface area
In Class E surface area, contact nearest tower, FSS, or Center
Special VFR clearances are only issued within surface areas (not en route)
AIM 4-4-6 is the reference for Special VFR procedures

questions.question: Can a “Special VFR clearance” be obtained into or out of Class B, C, D, or E airspace at night? (AIM 4-4-6) Special VFR operations by fixed-wing aircraft are prohibited between sunset and sunrise unless the pilot is instrument rated and the aircraft is equipped for IFR flight.
key_reference_points: Special VFR at night requires pilot to be instrument rated
Aircraft must be equipped for IFR flight for night Special VFR
Night Special VFR restriction applies between sunset and sunrise
Fixed-wing aircraft are prohibited from night Special VFR unless meeting both requirements
AIM 4-4-6 governs Special VFR operations

questions.question: What is a “Prohibited Area”? (AIM 3-4-2) Prohibited areas contain certain airspace of defined dimensions identified by an area on the surface of the earth within which the flight of aircraft is prohibited. Such areas are established for security or other reasons associated with the national welfare.
key_reference_points: Prohibited areas completely ban all aircraft flight within their boundaries
Established for security or national welfare reasons
Defined dimensions with surface area identification
Charted with a 'P' prefix followed by a number (e.g., P-56)
AIM 3-4-2 is the reference for Prohibited Areas
No flight is permitted without permission from the controlling agency

questions.question: What is a “Restricted Area”? (AIM 3-4-3) Restricted areas contain airspace identified by an area on the surface of the earth within which the flight of aircraft, while not wholly prohibited, is subject to restrictions. These areas denote the existence of unusual, often invisible, hazards to aircraft such as artillery firing, aerial gunnery, or guided missiles. Penetration of restricted areas without authorization from the using or controlling agency may be extremely hazardous to the aircraft and its occupants.
key_reference_points: Restricted areas contain airspace where flight is subject to restrictions, not wholly prohibited
Hazards include artillery firing, aerial gunnery, and guided missiles
Hazards are often invisible to pilots
Must obtain authorization from using or controlling agency before penetration
Identified on sectional charts with 'R' prefix and number

questions.question: Under what conditions, if any, may pilots enter restricted or prohibited areas? (14 CFR 91.133) No person may operate an aircraft within a restricted area contrary to the restrictions imposed, or within a prohibited area, unless that person has the permission of the using or controlling agency. Normally no operations are permitted within a prohibited area and prior permission must always be obtained before operating within a restricted area.
key_reference_points: Cannot operate in restricted area contrary to restrictions without permission
Cannot operate in prohibited area without permission
Permission must come from using or controlling agency
Normally NO operations permitted in prohibited areas
Prior permission ALWAYS required for restricted areas when active
Reference: 14 CFR 91.133

questions.question: What is a “Warning Area”? (AIM 3-4-4) A warning area is airspace of defined dimensions extending from three nautical miles outward from the coast of the United States, containing activity that may be hazardous to nonparticipating aircraft. The purpose of such an area is to warn nonparticipating pilots of the potential danger. A warning area may be located over domestic or international waters, or both.
key_reference_points: Warning areas extend from 3 NM outward from U.S. coastline
Contains activity hazardous to nonparticipating aircraft
May be over domestic or international waters or both
Purpose is to WARN pilots - entry not prohibited
Identified on charts with 'W' prefix and number
No ATC authorization required to enter

questions.question: What is a “MOA”? (AIM 3-4-5) A Military Operating Area (MOA) consists of airspace of defined vertical and lateral limits established for the purpose of separating certain military training activities from IFR traffic. Pilots operating under VFR should exercise extreme caution while flying within an MOA when military activity is being conducted. The activity status (active/inactive) of MOAs may change frequently. Therefore, pilots should contact any FSS within 100 miles of the area to obtain accurate real-time information concerning the MOA hours of operation. Prior to entering an active MOA, pilots should contact the controlling agency for traffic advisories.
key_reference_points: MOA = Military Operations Area, separates military training from IFR traffic
VFR pilots may enter but should exercise extreme caution when active
Contact FSS within 100 miles for real-time MOA activity status
Contact controlling agency for traffic advisories before entering active MOA
MOA activity status (active/inactive) changes frequently
Reference: AIM 3-4-5

questions.question: What is an “Alert Area”? (AIM 3-4-6) Alert areas are depicted on aeronautical charts to inform nonparticipating pilots of areas that may contain a high volume of pilot training or an unusual type of aerial activity. Pilots should be particularly alert when flying in these areas. All activity within an Alert Area shall be conducted in accordance with regulations, without waiver, and pilots of participating aircraft as well as pilots transiting the area shall be equally responsible for collision avoidance.
key_reference_points: Alert Areas warn of high volume pilot training or unusual aerial activity
No waiver required - all operations must comply with standard regulations
Both participating and transiting pilots equally responsible for collision avoidance
Depicted on aeronautical charts for pilot awareness
Reference: AIM 3-4-6

questions.question: What are “Controlled Firing Areas”? (AIM 3-4-7) Controlled Firing Areas (CFAs) contain activities that, if not conducted in a controlled environment, could be hazardous to nonparticipating aircraft. The distinguishing feature of the CFA, as compared to other special use airspace, is that its activities are suspended immediately when spotter aircraft, radar or ground lookout positions indicate an aircraft might be approaching the area. CFAs are not charted.
key_reference_points: CFAs contain potentially hazardous activities (e.g., weapons firing)
Activities suspended immediately when aircraft approach the area
Monitored by spotter aircraft, radar, or ground lookout positions
CFAs are NOT charted on aeronautical charts
No pilot action required - activity stops for you
Reference: AIM 3-4-7

questions.question: What is a “National Security Area”? (AIM 3-4-8) National Security Areas consist of airspace of defined vertical and lateral dimensions established at locations where there is a requirement for increased security and safety of ground facilities. Pilots are requested to voluntarily avoid flying through the depicted NSA. When is it necessary to provide a greater level of security and safety, flight in NSAs may be temporarily prohibited by regulation under the provisions of 14 CFR §99.7.
key_reference_points: NSAs are airspace areas requiring increased security for ground facilities
Avoidance is VOLUNTARY unless temporarily prohibited by regulation
Temporary prohibition authority comes from 14 CFR §99.7
NSAs are depicted on aeronautical charts with defined vertical and lateral dimensions
Unlike Prohibited/Restricted areas, NSAs do not normally restrict flight

questions.question: What is a Special Flight Rules Area (SFRA)? (14 CFR Part 93) An SFRA is an area of airspace within which Special Federal Aviation Regulations (SFARs) apply. Examples include the Washington D.C. SFRA and the Grand Canyon SFRA. Established operating requirements and procedures to operate within the SFRA can be found in 14 CFR Part 93 and on the specific chart legend for that area. Always check NOTAMs for possible procedural changes in the SFRA.
key_reference_points: SFRAs require compliance with Special Federal Aviation Regulations (SFARs)
Operating requirements found in 14 CFR Part 93
Washington D.C. SFRA and Grand Canyon SFRA are common examples
Must check chart legend for specific SFRA procedures
Always check NOTAMs for procedural changes in SFRAs
Washington D.C. SFRA requires specific training and flight plan

questions.question: Where can information on special use airspace be found? (AIM 3-4- 1) Special use airspace (except CFAs) are charted on IFR or visual charts and include the hours of operation, altitudes, and the controlling agency. (Additional information may be found at https://sua.faa.gov.)
key_reference_points: Special use airspace is charted on both IFR and VFR charts
Charts show hours of operation, altitudes, and controlling agency
Controlled Firing Areas (CFAs) are NOT charted
Additional SUA information available at sua.faa.gov
Reference AIM 3-4-1 for special use airspace details

questions.question: Where can a pilot find information on VFR flyways, VFR Corridors, and Class B airspace transition routes used to transition busy terminal airspace? (AIM 3-5-5) Information will normally be depicted on the reverse side of VFR Terminal Area Charts, commonly referred to as Class B airspace charts.
key_reference_points: VFR flyways, corridors, and transition routes are found on the reverse side of VFR Terminal Area Charts
These charts are commonly called Class B airspace charts
Used for transitioning busy terminal airspace without requiring ATC clearance (flyways) or with specific procedures (corridors/transition routes)
Reference: AIM 3-5-5

questions.question: What are “Military Training Routes”? (AIM 3-5-2) Military Training Routes are developed for use by the military for the purpose of conducting low-altitude, high speed training. The routes above 1,500 feet AGL are developed to be flown, to the maximum extent possible, under IFR. The routes at 1,500 feet AGL and below are generally developed to be flown under VFR. Routes below 1,500 feet AGL use four-digit identifiers (i.e. IR 1004, VR 1008). Routes above 1,500 feet AGL use three- digit identifiers, (i.e. IR 003, VR 004). IR is for IFR routes and VR is for VFR routes.
key_reference_points: Military Training Routes (MTRs) are for low-altitude, high-speed military training
Four-digit identifiers (IR 1004, VR 1008) = routes at or below 1,500 feet AGL
Three-digit identifiers (IR 003, VR 004) = routes above 1,500 feet AGL
IR = IFR routes, VR = VFR routes
Routes above 1,500 AGL are flown under IFR; routes at/below 1,500 AGL are flown under VFR
Reference: AIM 3-5-2

questions.question: What is a “TRSA”? (AIM Glossary) A Terminal Radar Service Area (TRSA) consists of airspace surrounding designated airports wherein ATC provides radar vectoring, sequencing, and separation on a full time basis for all IFR and participating VFR aircraft. Pilot participation is urged but not mandatory.
key_reference_points: TRSA = Terminal Radar Service Area
ATC provides radar vectoring, sequencing, and separation for IFR and participating VFR aircraft
Pilot participation is URGED but NOT MANDATORY (voluntary for VFR)
Service is provided on a full-time basis
TRSAs surround designated airports with radar approach control

questions.question: What class of airspace is a “TRSA”? (AIM 3-5-6) TRSAs do not fit into any of the U.S. airspace classes and are not contained in 14 CFR Part 71 nor are there any operating rules in Part 91. The primary airport(s) within the TRSA become Class D airspace. The remaining portion of a TRSA overlies other controlled airspace which is normally Class E airspace beginning at 700 or 1,200 feet and established to transition to/from the enroute/terminal environment. TRSAs will continue to be an airspace area where participating pilots can receive additional radar services which have been redefined as TRSA service.
key_reference_points: TRSAs do not fit into any defined U.S. airspace class
TRSAs are not contained in 14 CFR Part 71 and have no operating rules in Part 91
Primary airport(s) within TRSA become Class D airspace
Remaining TRSA area overlies Class E airspace (typically 700 or 1,200 feet AGL)
Participation is voluntary but provides additional radar services (TRSA service)
Purpose is to transition between enroute and terminal environments

questions.question: How are TRSAs depicted on navigational charts? (AIM 3-5-6) TRSAs are depicted on VFR sectional and terminal area charts with a solid black line and altitudes for each segment. The Class D portion is charted with a blue segmented line.
key_reference_points: TRSAs shown on VFR sectional and terminal area charts
Depicted with solid black line showing outer boundary
Altitudes listed for each segment within the TRSA
Class D portion within TRSA shown with blue segmented (dashed) line

questions.question: What are ADIZ and where are they located? (AIM Glossary) An Air Defense Identification Zone is an area of airspace over land or water, extending upward from the surface, within which the ready identification, the location and the control of aircraft are required in the interest of national security. ADIZ locations are: Domestic—located within U.S. along an international boundary. Coastal—located over coastal waters of the U.S. Distant Early Warning Identification Zone (DEWIZ)—located over coastal waters of the State of Alaska. Land-based ADIZ—located over U.S. metropolitan areas.
key_reference_points: ADIZ = Air Defense Identification Zone for national security purposes
Extends upward from the surface over land or water
Requires ready identification, location, and control of aircraft
Four types: Domestic (along international boundary), Coastal (over U.S. coastal waters), DEWIZ (Alaska coastal waters), Land-based (over U.S. metropolitan areas)
Flight plan and two-way radio communication required before entry

questions.question: What requirements must be satisfied prior to operations into, within or across an ADIZ? (AIM 5-6-1) Operational requirements for aircraft operations associated with an ADIZ are as follows: Flight plan—An IFR or DVFR flight plan must be filed with the appropriate aeronautical facility. Two-way radio—An operating two-way radio is required. Transponder—Aircraft must be equipped with an operable radar beacon transponder having altitude reporting (Mode C) capabilities. The transponder must be turned on and set to the assigned ATC code. Position reports—For IFR flights, normal position reporting. For DVFR flights, an estimated time of ADIZ penetration must be filed at least 15 minutes prior to entry. Aircraft position tolerances—Over land, a tolerance of ±5 minutes from the estimated time over a reporting point and within 10 NM from the centerline of an intended track over an estimated reporting point. Over water, a tolerance of ±5 minutes from the estimated time over a reporting point or point of penetration and within 20 NM from centerline of an intended track over an estimated reporting point. Land-based ADIZ—are activated and deactivated over U.S. metropolitan areas as needed, with dimensions, activation dates, etc., disseminated via NOTAM. In addition to other ADIZ requirements, pilots must report landing or leaving the land-based ADIZ if flying too low for radar coverage.
questions.answer: F. Airspace Classification Summary The following section summarizes the requirements for operations within the various airspace classes.
key_reference_points: File IFR or DVFR flight plan before ADIZ operations
Two-way radio communication required
Mode C transponder required - must be on and set to assigned code
DVFR flights: file estimated ADIZ penetration time at least 15 minutes prior to entry
Position tolerance over land: ±5 minutes and within 10 NM of centerline
Position tolerance over water: ±5 minutes and within 20 NM of centerline

questions.question: Discuss “Class A” airspace. Vertical dimensions: 18,000 feet MSL up to and include FL600 Operations permitted: IFR Entry prerequisites: ATC Clearance Minimum pilot qualifications: Instrument rating Two-way radio communications: Yes VFR minimum visibility: N/A VFR minimum distance from clouds: N/A Aircraft separation: All Conflict resolution: N/A Traffic advisories: N/A Safety advisories: Yes
key_reference_points: Class A: 18,000 feet MSL to FL600 (inclusive)
IFR operations ONLY - no VFR permitted
ATC clearance required for entry
Instrument rating required for pilot
Two-way radio required
ATC provides separation for ALL aircraft

questions.question: Discuss “Class B” airspace. Vertical dimensions: Surface to 10,000 feet MSL Operations permitted: IFR and VFR Entry prerequisites: ATC clearance Minimum pilot qualifications: Private/Student Two-way radio communications: Yes VFR Minimum visibility: 3 statute miles VFR Minimum distance from clouds: Clear of clouds Aircraft separation: All Conflict resolution: Yes Traffic advisories: Yes Safety advisories: Yes
key_reference_points: Class B: Surface to 10,000 feet MSL (individually tailored)
ATC clearance required before entry (hear your callsign)
Private pilot certificate OR student pilot with specific endorsement
VFR: 3 SM visibility, clear of clouds
Two-way radio and Mode C transponder required
ATC separates ALL aircraft (IFR and VFR)

questions.question: Discuss “Class C” airspace. Vertical dimensions: Surface to 4,000 feet AGL (charted MSL) Operations permitted: IFR and VFR Entry prerequisites: ATC clearance for IFR; radio contact for all Minimum pilot qualifications: Student certificate Two-way radio communications: Yes VFR Minimum visibility: 3 statute miles VFR Minimum distance from clouds: 500' below, 1,000' above, and 2,000' horizontal Aircraft separation: IFR, SVFR and runway operations Conflict resolution: Between IFR and VFR operations Traffic advisories: Yes Safety advisories: Yes
key_reference_points: Class C extends from surface to 4,000 feet AGL (charted in MSL)
Two-way radio communication required before entry - must establish contact with ATC
VFR weather minimums: 3 SM visibility, 500' below, 1,000' above, 2,000' horizontal from clouds
ATC provides separation for IFR, SVFR, and runway operations
ATC provides conflict resolution between IFR and VFR aircraft
Traffic advisories and safety advisories are provided to all aircraft

questions.question: Discuss “Class D” airspace. Vertical dimensions: Surface to 2,500 feet AGL (charted MSL) Operations permitted: IFR and VFR Entry prerequisites: ATC clearance for IFR; radio contact for all Minimum pilot qualifications: Student certificate Two-way radio communications: Yes VFR Minimum visibility: 3 statute miles VFR Minimum distance from clouds: 500' below, 1,000' above, and 2,000' horizontal Aircraft separation: IFR, SVFR and runway operations Conflict resolution: No Traffic advisories: Workload permitting Safety advisories: Yes
key_reference_points: Class D extends from surface to 2,500 feet AGL (charted in MSL)
Two-way radio communication must be established before entry
VFR weather minimums: 3 SM visibility, 500' below, 1,000' above, 2,000' horizontal from clouds
ATC provides separation only for IFR, SVFR, and runway operations
NO conflict resolution service provided (unlike Class C)
Traffic advisories provided only workload permitting

questions.question: Discuss “Class E” airspace. Vertical dimensions: Except for 18,000 feet MSL, no defined vertical limit. Extends upward from either the surface or a designated altitude to the overlying or adjacent controlled airspace. Operations permitted: IFR and VFR Entry prerequisites: ATC clearance for IFR Minimum pilot qualifications: Student certificate Two-way radio communications: Yes for IFR VFR minimum visibility: *3 statute miles VFR minimum distance from clouds *500' below, 1,000' above, and 2,000' horizontal Aircraft separation: IFR and SVFR Conflict resolution: No Traffic advisories: Workload permitting Safety advisories: Yes *Different visibility minima and distance cloud requirements exist for operations above 10,000 feet MSL and Special VFR.
key_reference_points: Class E has no defined vertical limit - extends from surface or designated altitude up to 18,000 feet MSL
No radio communication or ATC contact required for VFR flight
Below 10,000 MSL: 3 SM visibility, 500' below, 1,000' above, 2,000' horizontal from clouds
At/above 10,000 MSL: 5 SM visibility, 1,000' below, 1,000' above, 1 SM horizontal from clouds
Surface-based Class E depicted with dashed magenta line on charts
ATC separation provided only for IFR and SVFR aircraft

questions.question: Discuss “Class G” airspace. Vertical dimensions: Surface up to the overlying controlled (Class E) airspace, not to exceed 14,500 feet MSL Operations permitted: IFR and VFR Entry prerequisites: None Minimum pilot qualifications: Student certificate Two-way radio communications: No VFR minimum visibility: *1 statute mile VFR minimum distance from clouds: *500' below, 1,000' above and 2,000' horizontal Aircraft separation: None Conflict resolution: No Traffic advisories: Workload permitting Safety advisories: Yes *Different visibility minima and distance from cloud requirements exist for night operations, operations above 10,000 feet MSL, and operations below 1,200 feet AGL.
key_reference_points: Class G extends from surface up to overlying Class E, never exceeding 14,500 feet MSL
No entry requirements, no radio required, no ATC separation provided
Basic VFR minimums: 1 SM visibility, 500' below/1,000' above/2,000' horizontal from clouds
Minimum pilot certificate required: Student pilot certificate
Both IFR and VFR operations are permitted
Different visibility/cloud clearance rules apply for night, above 10,000 MSL, and below 1,200 AGL

questions.question: When is immediate notification to the NTSB required? (NTSB Part 830.5) The operator of an aircraft shall immediately, and by the most expeditious means available, notify the nearest NTSB office when an aircraft accident or any of the following listed serious incidents occur:
questions.answer: a. Flight control system malfunction b. Crewmember unable to perform normal duties c. Inflight fire d. Aircraft collision inflight e. Property damage, other than aircraft, estimated to exceed $25,000 f. Overdue aircraft (believed to be in accident) g. Release of all or a portion of a propeller blade from an aircraft h. Complete loss of information (excluding flickering), from more than 50 percent of an aircraft’s EFIS cockpit displays.
key_reference_points: NTSB Part 830.5 requires IMMEDIATE notification by most expeditious means
Flight control malfunction or crewmember unable to perform duties requires notification
Inflight fire or inflight collision requires immediate NTSB notification
Property damage (other than aircraft) exceeding $25,000 requires notification
Overdue aircraft believed to be in an accident must be reported
Loss of more than 50% of EFIS displays or propeller blade release requires notification

questions.question: Define “aircraft incident.” (NTSB Part 830.2) An aircraft incident means an occurrence other than an accident associated with the operation of an aircraft, which affects or could affect the safety of operations.
key_reference_points: Defined in NTSB Part 830.2
An incident is NOT an accident but is associated with aircraft operation
Key distinction: affects or COULD affect safety of operations
Incidents are less severe than accidents but still reportable under certain conditions

questions.question: Define “aircraft accident.” (NTSB Part 830.2) An aircraft accident means an occurrence associated with the operation of an aircraft which takes place between the time any person boards the aircraft with the intention of flight and all such persons have disembarked, and in which any person suffers death or serious injury, or in which the aircraft receives substantial damage.
key_reference_points: Occurs between boarding with intent to fly and disembarkation of all persons
Any person suffers death or serious injury
Aircraft receives substantial damage
All three elements define the timeframe and outcomes that constitute an accident

questions.question: Define the term “serious injury.” (NTSB Part 830.2) Serious injury means any injury that:
questions.answer: a. Requires hospitalization for more than 48 hours, commencing within 7 days from the date the injury was received; b. Results in a fracture of any bone (except simple fractures of fingers, toes or nose); c. Causes severe hemorrhages, nerve, muscle or tendon damage; d. Involves any internal organ; or e. Involves second- or third-degree burns affecting more than 5% of the body surface.
key_reference_points: Hospitalization more than 48 hours, commencing within 7 days of injury
Fracture of any bone EXCEPT simple fractures of fingers, toes, or nose
Severe hemorrhage or nerve/muscle/tendon damage
Injury involving any internal organ
Second or third-degree burns affecting more than 5% of body surface

questions.question: Define the term “substantial damage.” (NTSB Part 830.2) “Substantial damage” means damage or failure which adversely affects the structural strength, performance or flight characteristics of the aircraft and which would normally require major repair or replacement of the affected component. Engine failure or damage limited to an engine if only one engine fails or is damaged; bent fairings or cowling; dented skin; small punctured holes in the skin or fabric; ground damage to rotor or propeller blades; and damage to landing gear, wheels, tires, flaps, engine accessories, brakes, or wing tips are not considered substantial damage for the purpose of this part.
key_reference_points: Adversely affects structural strength, performance, or flight characteristics
Normally requires major repair or replacement of affected component
NOT substantial: engine failure/damage limited to one engine
NOT substantial: bent fairings, cowling, dented skin, small punctures
NOT substantial: ground damage to rotor/propeller blades
NOT substantial: damage to landing gear, wheels, tires, flaps, brakes, or wingtips

questions.question: Will notification to the NTSB always be necessary in any aircraft “accident” even if there were no injuries? (NTSB Part 830) Refer to the definition of “Accident.” An aircraft accident can involve substantial damage and/or injuries, and the NTSB always requires a report if this is the case.
key_reference_points: NTSB defines 'accident' as an occurrence involving substantial damage to aircraft OR death/serious injury
Notification is ALWAYS required for any aircraft accident regardless of injuries
Substantial damage alone (even with no injuries) triggers mandatory NTSB notification
NTSB Part 830 governs accident/incident reporting requirements

questions.question: Where are accident or incident reports filed? (NTSB Part 830) The operator of an aircraft shall file any report with the field office of the Board nearest the accident or incident. The National Transportation Safety Board field offices are listed in the U.S. government pages of telephone directories in major cities.
key_reference_points: Reports filed with NTSB field office nearest to the accident/incident location
NTSB field offices listed in government pages of telephone directories in major cities
The aircraft OPERATOR is responsible for filing the report
Reports can also be filed online through NTSB website

questions.question: After an accident or incident has occurred, how soon must a report be filed with the NTSB? (NTSB Part 830) The operator shall file a report on NTSB Form 6120.1/2, available from NTSB field offices, the NTSB in Washington D.C., or the FAA Flight Standards District Office:
questions.answer: a. Within 10 days after an accident; b. When, after 7 days, an overdue aircraft is still missing.
key_reference_points: Accident report must be filed within 10 days
Overdue aircraft still missing after 7 days requires a report to be filed
Use NTSB Form 6120.1/2 for filing reports
Forms available from NTSB field offices, NTSB Washington D.C., or FAA FSDO
The aircraft OPERATOR is responsible for filing the report
questions.note: A report on an “Incident” for which notification is required as described shall be filed only as requested by an authorized representative of the NTSB.

questions.question: Can the FAA use reports submitted to NASA for enforcement purposes? (14 CFR 91.25; AC 00-46) The FAA will not use reports submitted to NASA under the Aviation Safety Reporting Program (or information derived therefrom) in any enforcement action except information concerning accidents or criminal offenses which are wholly excluded from the program. By submitting a report within 10 days following an incident, the pilot is not protected from the FAA finding a violation of regulation, but may be providing himself some immunity from a civil penalty or possible suspension of certificate.
key_reference_points: FAA cannot use NASA ASRS reports for enforcement except for accidents or criminal offenses
Report must be submitted within 10 days of the incident
Filing does NOT prevent FAA from finding a violation occurred
Filing provides immunity from civil penalty or certificate suspension
Reference: 14 CFR 91.25 and AC 00-46

questions.question: What type of aeronautical lighting is “VASI”? (AIM 2-1-2) Visual Approach Slope Indicator (VASI) is a system of lights so arranged to provide visual descent guidance information during the approach to a runway. The basic principle of VASI is that of color differential between red and white: each light projects a beam of light having a white segment in the upper half and a red segment in the lower part of the beam. The lights in a two-bar VASI will be as follows: Red Over Red—below Glide Path Red Over White—on Glide Path White Over White—above Glide Path
key_reference_points: VASI = Visual Approach Slope Indicator
Uses color differential between red and white light segments
Red Over Red = Below glide path (too low)
Red Over White = On glide path (correct)
White Over White = Above glide path (too high)
Memory aid: Red over white, you're alright; red over red, you're dead

questions.question: What is “PAPI”? (AIM 2-1-2) The Precision Approach Path Indicator (PAPI) uses light units similar to the VASI, but are installed in a single row of either two- or four-light units. These systems have an effective visual range of about 5 miles during the day and up to 20 miles at night. The row of light units are normally installed on the left side of the runway. Four white lights: High (More than 3.5 degrees) Three white one red: Slightly high (3.2 degrees) Two white two red: On glide path (3 degrees) One white three red: Slightly low (2.8 degrees) Four red lights: Low (Less than 2.5 degrees)
key_reference_points: PAPI = Precision Approach Path Indicator
Single row of 2 or 4 light units, usually on left side of runway
Four white = Too high (>3.5°)
Two white/two red = On glide path (3°)
Four red = Too low (<2.5°)
Visual range: ~5 miles day, up to 20 miles night

questions.question: What does the operation of an airport rotating beacon during the hours of daylight indicate? (AIM 2-1-10) In Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas, operation of the airport beacon during the hours of daylight often indicates that the ground visibility is less than 3 miles and/or the ceiling is less than 1,000 feet. ATC clearance in accordance with 14 CFR Part 91 is required for landing, takeoff and flight in the traffic pattern. Pilots should not rely solely on the operation of the airport beacon to indicate if weather conditions are IFR or VFR. There is no regulatory requirement for daylight operation and it is the pilot’s responsibility to comply with proper preflight planning as required by 14 CFR Part 91.
key_reference_points: Daylight beacon operation indicates ground visibility less than 3 miles and/or ceiling less than 1,000 feet
Applies to Class B, C, D, and E surface areas
ATC clearance required for landing, takeoff, and traffic pattern operations when beacon is on during daylight
No regulatory requirement for daylight beacon operation - pilots must verify actual weather conditions
Pilot responsibility to conduct proper preflight planning per 14 CFR Part 91

questions.question: What are the six types of signs installed at airports? (AIM 2-3-8 through 2-3-13)
questions.answer: a. Mandatory instruction sign—red background/white inscription; denotes an entrance to a runway, a critical area, or a prohibited area. b. Location sign—black background/yellow inscription/yellow border; do not have arrows; used to identify a taxiway or runway location, the boundary of the runway, or identify an ILS critical area. c. Direction sign—yellow background/black inscription; identifies the designation of the intersecting taxiway(s) leading out of an intersection that a pilot would expect to turn onto or hold short of. d. Destination sign—yellow background/black inscription and also contain arrows; provides information on locating runways, terminals, cargo areas, and civil aviation areas, etc. e. Information sign—yellow background/black inscription; used to provide the pilot with information on areas that can’t be seen from the control tower, applicable radio frequencies, and noise abatement procedures, etc. f. Runway distance remaining sign—black background/white numeral inscription; indicates the distance of the remaining runway in thousands of feet.
key_reference_points: Mandatory instruction signs: RED background/WHITE inscription - runway entrances, critical areas, prohibited areas
Location signs: BLACK background/YELLOW inscription/YELLOW border - identifies current taxiway or runway location
Direction signs: YELLOW background/BLACK inscription - shows intersecting taxiways with expected turns
Destination signs: YELLOW background/BLACK inscription WITH ARROWS - points to runways, terminals, cargo areas
Information signs: YELLOW background/BLACK inscription - radio frequencies, noise abatement, areas not visible from tower
Runway distance remaining signs: BLACK background/WHITE numerals - shows remaining runway in thousands of feet

questions.question: What color are runway markings? Taxiway markings? (AIM 2-3-1) Markings for runways are white. Markings for taxiways, areas not intended for use by aircraft (closed and hazardous areas), and holding positions (even if they are on a runway) are yellow.
key_reference_points: Runway markings are WHITE
