Taxiway markings are YELLOW
Closed and hazardous area markings are YELLOW
Holding position markings are YELLOW (even when located on a runway)

questions.question: What airport marking aids will be used to indicate the following? (AIM 2-3-3, 2-3-5, 2-3-6) Runway Threshold Markings—These come in two configurations. They either consist of eight longitudinal stripes of uniform dimensions disposed symmetrically about the runway centerline, or the number of stripes is related to the runway width. A threshold marking helps identify the beginning of the runway available for landing. Displaced Threshold—A threshold located at a point on the runway other than the designated beginning of the runway. A displaced threshold reduces the length of runway available for landings. The portion of runway behind a displaced threshold is available for takeoffs in either direction. A ten-foot wide white threshold bar is located across the width of the runway at the displaced threshold. White arrows are located along the centerline in the area between the beginning of the runway and displaced threshold. White arrowheads are located across the width of the runway just prior to the threshold bar. Runway Hold Position Markings—For taxiways, these markings indicate where an aircraft is supposed to stop when it does not have clearance to proceed onto the runway. They are also installed on runways only if the runway is normally used by air traffic control for “land, hold short” operations. They consist of four yellow lines, two solid and two dashed, spaced six inches apart and extending across the width of the taxiway or runway. Temporarily closed runways and taxiways—Provides a visual indication to pilots that a runway/taxiway is temporarily closed. Yellow crosses are placed on the runway only at each end of the runway. Closed taxiways are blocked with barricades or may utilize a yellow cross at the entrance to the taxiway. Permanently closed runways and taxiways—For runways and taxiways which are permanently closed, the lighting circuits will be disconnected. The runway threshold, runway designation, and touchdown markings are obliterated and yellow crosses are placed at each end of the runway and at 1,000-foot intervals.
key_reference_points: Runway threshold markings: 8 longitudinal white stripes symmetrically placed about centerline
Displaced threshold: 10-foot white threshold bar across runway width, white arrows on centerline, white arrowheads before the bar
Displaced threshold area available for takeoffs but NOT for landing
Runway hold position markings: 4 yellow lines (2 solid, 2 dashed) across taxiway width - stop when solid lines are on your side
Temporarily closed runways: Yellow X placed at each end of runway only
Permanently closed runways: Markings obliterated, lighting disconnected, yellow X at each end AND at 1,000-foot intervals

questions.question: What are the different methods a pilot may use to determine the proper runway and traffic pattern in use at an airport without an operating control tower? (AIM 4-1-9, 4-3-4)
questions.answer: a. At an airport with a full or part-time UNICOM station in operation, an advisory may be obtained which will usually include wind direction and velocity, favored or designated runway, right or left traffic, altimeter setting, known traffic, NOTAMs, etc. b. Many airports are now providing completely automated weather, radio check capability and airport advisory information on an automated UNICOM system. Availability of the automated UNICOM will be published in the Chart Supplement U.S. and approach charts. c. At those airports where these services are not available, a segmented circle visual indicator system, if installed, is designated to provide traffic pattern information. The segmented circle system consists of the following components: • The segmented circle • The wind direction indicator (wind sock, cone, or tee) • The landing direction indicator (a tetrahedron) • Landing strip indicators • Traffic pattern indicators
key_reference_points: UNICOM advisory provides wind, runway in use, traffic pattern direction, altimeter, and traffic information
Automated UNICOM systems published in Chart Supplement U.S. and approach charts
Segmented circle system components: segmented circle, wind direction indicator (windsock/cone/tee), landing direction indicator (tetrahedron), landing strip indicators, traffic pattern indicators
Wind sock/cone shows wind direction; tetrahedron points into the wind indicating landing direction
Traffic pattern indicators show left or right traffic pattern for each runway

questions.question: What is the standard direction of turns when approaching an uncontrolled airport for landing? (AIM 4-3-3) When approaching for landing, all turns must be made to the left unless a traffic pattern indicator indicates that turns should be made to the right.
key_reference_points: Standard traffic pattern turns are always LEFT turns
Right traffic only when indicated by traffic pattern indicator (segmented circle)
Right traffic patterns published in Chart Supplement U.S.
Check for visual indicators at unfamiliar uncontrolled airports

questions.question: What is considered standard for traffic pattern altitude? (AIM 4-3-3) Unless otherwise established, 1,000 feet AGL is the recommended traffic pattern altitude. At most airports and military air bases, traffic pattern altitudes for propeller-driven aircraft generally extend from 600 feet to as high as 1,500 feet AGL. Also, traffic pattern altitudes for military turbojet aircraft sometimes extend up to 2,500 feet AGL.
key_reference_points: Standard traffic pattern altitude is 1,000 feet AGL unless otherwise established
Propeller-driven aircraft patterns range from 600 to 1,500 feet AGL
Military turbojet aircraft patterns may extend up to 2,500 feet AGL
Reference: AIM 4-3-3

questions.question: What recommended entry and departure procedures should be used at airports without an operating control tower? (AIM 4-3-3) A pilot should plan to enter the traffic pattern in level flight, abeam the midpoint of the runway at pattern altitude. When departing a traffic pattern, continue straight out, or exit with a 45-degree turn (to the left when in a left- hand traffic pattern; to the right when in a right-hand traffic pattern) beyond the departure end of the runway, after reaching pattern altitude.
key_reference_points: Enter traffic pattern level flight, abeam midpoint of runway, at pattern altitude
Depart by continuing straight out OR 45-degree turn in direction of traffic pattern
Left turn departure for left-hand pattern; right turn for right-hand pattern
Turn after reaching pattern altitude and beyond departure end of runway
Reference: AIM 4-3-3

questions.question: If in doubt about the traffic pattern altitude for a particular airport, what publication can provide this information? The Chart Supplement U.S.
key_reference_points: Chart Supplement U.S. (formerly Airport/Facility Directory) contains traffic pattern altitude information
Use Chart Supplement when traffic pattern altitude is non-standard or unknown
Chart Supplement provides airport-specific operational information

questions.question: What is an “ARTCC,” and what useful service can it provide to VFR flights? (AIM Glossary) An “Air Route Traffic Control Center” is a facility established to provide air traffic control service primarily to aircraft operating on IFR flight plans within controlled airspace and principally during the en route phase of flight. Air Route Surveillance Radar allows them the capability to detect and display an aircraft’s position while en route between terminal areas. When equipment capabilities and controller workload permit, certain advisory/assistance service may be provided to VFR aircraft (VFR Flight Following). Frequencies may be obtained from FSS or the Chart Supplement U.S. Also, IFR enroute charts have ARTCC sector frequencies depicted. If departing from an airport with a control tower, you can request the appropriate frequency from them.
key_reference_points: ARTCC stands for Air Route Traffic Control Center
Primary function is controlling IFR traffic in controlled airspace during en route phase
Uses Air Route Surveillance Radar to detect and display aircraft positions
Can provide VFR Flight Following when workload permits
ARTCC frequencies found in Chart Supplement U.S., from FSS, IFR enroute charts, or control towers

questions.question: What are the following transponder codes? (AIM 4-1-20, 6-4-2) 1200—VFR operations 7500—Hijack 7600—Communications failure 7700—Emergency
key_reference_points: 1200 = VFR operations (standard VFR squawk code)
7500 = Hijack (think '75 - taken alive')
7600 = Communications failure (think '76 - need a fix')
7700 = Emergency (think '77 - going to heaven')
Emergency codes (75, 76, 77) trigger immediate ATC attention

questions.question: When conducting flight operations into an airport with an operating control tower, when should initial contact be established? (AIM 4-3-2) When operating at an airport where traffic control is being exercised by a control tower, pilots are required to maintain two-way radio contact with the tower while operating within Class B, Class C, and Class D surface areas, unless the tower authorizes otherwise. Initial call-up should be made about 15 miles from the airport. Also, not all airports with an operating control tower will have Class D airspace. These airports do not have weather reporting, which is a requirement for surface-based controlled airspace. Pilots are expected to use good operating practices and communicate with the control tower.
key_reference_points: Two-way radio contact required in Class B, C, and D surface areas
Initial contact should be made approximately 15 miles from the airport
Some towered airports may not have Class D airspace (no weather reporting capability)
Must maintain contact unless tower authorizes otherwise
Use good operating practices even at non-Class D towered airports

questions.question: What communication procedures are recommended when departing a Class D airspace area? (AIM 4-3-2) Unless there is good reason to leave the tower frequency before exiting the Class B, Class C and Class D surface areas, it is good operating practice to remain on the tower frequency for the purpose of receiving traffic information. In the interest of reducing tower frequency congestion, pilots are reminded that it is not necessary to request permission to leave the tower frequency once outside of Class B, Class C, and Class D surface areas.
key_reference_points: Remain on tower frequency until exiting Class B, C, or D surface areas unless there is good reason to leave
No permission required to leave tower frequency once outside Class B, C, or D surface areas
Staying on frequency allows receipt of traffic information
Leaving frequency without necessity contributes to tower frequency congestion

questions.question: How do you convert from standard time to coordinated universal time? (AIM 4-2-12) You should take the local time (converted to military time) and add the time differential to convert to UTC. Eastern Standard Time: add 5 hours Central Standard Time: add 6 hours Mountain Standard Time: add 7 hours Pacific Standard Time: add 8 hours Alaska Standard Time: add 9 hours Hawaii Standard Time: add 10 hours
key_reference_points: Eastern Standard Time: add 5 hours to local time for UTC
Central Standard Time: add 6 hours to local time for UTC
Mountain Standard Time: add 7 hours to local time for UTC
Pacific Standard Time: add 8 hours to local time for UTC
Alaska Standard Time: add 9 hours to local time for UTC
Hawaii Standard Time: add 10 hours to local time for UTC
questions.note: For Daylight Savings Time subtract 1 hour from above.

questions.question: Arrange the radio facilities listed below in the order they would be used when operating into or out of a tower controlled airport within Class B, C, or D airspace. Approach Control ATIS Ground Control Control Tower Clearance Delivery Departure Control Arriving Aircraft: ATIS, Approach Control, Control Tower, Ground Control Departing Aircraft: ATIS, Clearance Delivery (if required for the surrounding airspace, i.e., Class B, C or D airspace), Ground Control, Control Tower, Departure Control.
key_reference_points: Arriving sequence: ATIS → Approach Control → Control Tower → Ground Control
Departing sequence: ATIS → Clearance Delivery → Ground Control → Control Tower → Departure Control
Always obtain ATIS first for both arrivals and departures
Clearance Delivery is required when departing Class B, C, or D airspace
Ground Control handles taxi instructions; Tower handles runway operations

questions.question: What are “NOTAMs”? (AIM 5-1-3) Notices To Airmen (NOTAM)—time-critical aeronautical information of either a temporary nature, or not known sufficiently in advance to permit publication on aeronautical charts or in other operational publications, receives immediate dissemination via the National NOTAM System. This is aeronautical information that could affect a pilot’s decision to make a flight. It includes such information as airport or primary runway closures, changes in the status of navigational aids, ILS’s, radar service availability, and other information essential to planned en route, terminal, or landing operations. NOTAMs can be viewed via the PilotWeb site: https://pilotweb.nas.faa.gov/PilotWeb/
key_reference_points: NOTAMs provide time-critical aeronautical information of a temporary nature
Information not known far enough in advance for publication on charts or in operational publications
Disseminated via the National NOTAM System
Includes airport/runway closures, NAVAID status changes, ILS changes, radar service availability
Can affect a pilot's decision to make a flight
Accessible via PilotWeb: https://pilotweb.nas.faa.gov/PilotWeb/

questions.question: What are the five categories of NOTAMs? (AIM 5-1-3) NOTAMs are classified into five categories:
questions.answer: a. (D) NOTAMs—Information that requires wide dissemination via telecommunication and pertains to en route navigational aids, civil public- use airports listed in the AFD, facilities, services, and procedures. b. FDC NOTAMs—Flight information that is regulatory in nature including, but not limited to, changes to IFR charts, procedures, and airspace usage. c. POINTER NOTAMs—issued by a flight service station to highlight or point out another NOTAM; such as an FDC NOTAM. These NOTAMs assist users in cross-referencing important information that may not be found under an airport or NAVAID identifier. d. SAA NOTAMs—are issued when Special Activity Airspace will be active outside the published schedule times and when required by the published schedule. e. MILITARY NOTAMs—pertain to U.S. Air Force, Army, Marine, and Navy navigational aids/airports that are part of the NAS.
key_reference_points: (D) NOTAMs - Wide dissemination for en route NAVAIDs, public-use airports, facilities, services, procedures
FDC NOTAMs - Regulatory information including IFR chart/procedure/airspace changes
POINTER NOTAMs - Issued by FSS to cross-reference or highlight other NOTAMs
SAA NOTAMs - Special Activity Airspace active outside published schedule times
MILITARY NOTAMs - Pertain to Air Force, Army, Marine, and Navy NAVAIDs/airports in the NAS

questions.question: All (D) NOTAMS will have keywords contained within the first part of the text. What are several examples of these keywords? (AIM 5-1-3) RWY, TWY, RAMP, APRON, AD, OBST, NAV, COM, SVC, AIRSPACE, (U) unverified, (O) other
key_reference_points: Keywords appear in the first part of (D) NOTAM text
Common keywords: RWY, TWY, RAMP, APRON, AD, OBST, NAV, COM, SVC, AIRSPACE
(U) indicates unverified information
(O) indicates other/miscellaneous information
Keywords help pilots quickly identify the subject matter of the NOTAM

questions.question: What is a “TFR”? (AC 91-63) A temporary flight restriction (TFR) is a regulatory action issued via the U.S. NOTAM system to restrict certain aircraft from operating within a defined area, on a temporary basis, to protect persons or property in the air or on the ground. They may be issued due to a hazardous condition, a special event, or as a general warning for the entire FAA airspace. TFR information can be obtained from an AFSS or on the Internet at www.faa.gov.
key_reference_points: TFR stands for Temporary Flight Restriction
Issued through the U.S. NOTAM system
Restricts aircraft from operating within a defined area temporarily
Purposes: protect persons/property, hazardous conditions, special events, or general warnings
TFR information available from AFSS or www.faa.gov
questions.exam_tip: On the day of your practical test, verify that a last minute TFR hasn’t been issued for your area or along your planned route of flight.

questions.question: Where can NOTAM information be obtained? (AIM 5-1-3)
questions.answer: a. Flight Service Station b. Notice to Airman Publication (NTAP)—printed NOTAMs c. DUATs vendors d. Internet website—www.faa.gov/pilots/flt_plan/notams/ e. Broadcast Flight Information Services (FIS-B)—displayed in cockpit
key_reference_points: Flight Service Station (FSS) provides NOTAM briefings
Notice to Airman Publication (NTAP) contains printed NOTAMs
DUATS vendors provide NOTAM access
FAA website: www.faa.gov/pilots/flt_plan/notams/
FIS-B (Flight Information Services-Broadcast) displays NOTAMs in cockpit

questions.question: When are VFR flight plans required to be filed? (AIM 5-1-4) Except for operations in or penetrating a Coastal or Domestic ADIZ or DEWIZ, a flight plan is not required for VFR flight; however, it is strongly recommended that one be filed with an FAA FSS when making extended cross-country flights. This will ensure that you receive VFR Search and Rescue Protection.
key_reference_points: VFR flight plans are NOT required except for ADIZ/DEWIZ penetration
Strongly recommended for extended cross-country flights
File with FAA Flight Service Station (FSS)
Filing activates VFR Search and Rescue Protection
ADIZ = Air Defense Identification Zone, DEWIZ = Distant Early Warning Identification Zone

questions.question: What is a DVFR flight plan? (AIM 5-1-6) Defense VFR; VFR flights into a Coastal or Domestic ADIZ/DEWIZ are required to file VFR flight plans for security purposes. The flight plan must be filed before departure.
key_reference_points: DVFR = Defense VFR flight plan
Required for all VFR flights into Coastal or Domestic ADIZ/DEWIZ
Purpose is national security/defense identification
Must be filed BEFORE departure
Reference: AIM 5-1-6

questions.question: When you land at an airport with an ATC tower in operation will the tower automatically close your flight plan? (AIM 5-1-14) Control towers do not automatically close VFR or DVFR flight plans since they do not know if a particular VFR aircraft is on a flight plan. A pilot is responsible for ensuring that his/her VFR or DVFR flight plan is canceled. You should close your flight plan with the nearest FSS, or if one is not available, you may request any ATC facility to relay your cancellation.
key_reference_points: ATC towers do NOT automatically close VFR or DVFR flight plans
Towers cannot distinguish if a VFR aircraft is on a flight plan
Pilot is responsible for canceling their own VFR/DVFR flight plan
Close flight plan with nearest FSS or request ATC to relay cancellation
Reference: AIM 5-1-14

questions.question: If your flight is behind schedule, and you do not report the delay, or you forget to close your flight plan, how much time from ETA does the FSS allow before search and rescue efforts are begun? (AIM 5-1-14) If you fail to report or cancel your flight plan within one-half hour after your ETA, Search and Rescue procedures are started.
key_reference_points: Search and Rescue begins 30 minutes (1/2 hour) after ETA if no contact
Pilot must report delays or cancel flight plan to prevent SAR activation
Failure to close flight plan triggers unnecessary search operations
Reference: AIM 5-1-14

questions.question: What is wake turbulence? (AIM Glossary) A phenomenon resulting from the passage of an aircraft through the atmosphere. The term includes vortices, thrust stream turbulence, jet blast, jet wash, propeller wash, and rotor wash, both on the ground and in the air.
key_reference_points: Wake turbulence includes vortices, thrust stream turbulence, jet blast, jet wash, propeller wash, and rotor wash
Occurs both on the ground and in the air
Results from any aircraft passing through the atmosphere

questions.question: Where are wake turbulence and wingtip vortices likely to occur? (AIM 7-3-3) All aircraft generate turbulence and associated wingtip vortices. In general, avoid the area behind and below the generating aircraft, especially at low altitudes. Also of concern is the weight, speed, and shape of the wing of the generating aircraft. The greatest vortex strength occurs when the generating aircraft is HEAVY, CLEAN and SLOW.
key_reference_points: Avoid the area BEHIND and BELOW the generating aircraft
Greatest vortex strength when aircraft is HEAVY, CLEAN, and SLOW
Most dangerous at low altitudes
All aircraft generate wake turbulence regardless of size
Wing weight, speed, and shape affect vortex intensity

questions.question: What operational procedures should be followed when wake vortices are suspected to exist? (AIM 7-3-6)
questions.answer: a. Landing behind a larger aircraft on the same runway—Stay at or above the larger aircraft’s final approach flight path. Note its touchdown point and land beyond it. b. Landing behind a larger aircraft, when parallel runway is closer than 2,500 feet—Consider possible drift to your runway. Stay at or above the larger aircraft’s final approach flight path, and note its touchdown point. c. Landing behind a larger aircraft, crossing runway—Cross above the larger aircraft’s flight path. d. Landing behind a departing larger aircraft on the same runway—Note the larger aircraft’s rotation point, and land well prior to rotation point. e. Landing behind a departing larger aircraft, crossing runway—Note the larger aircraft’s rotation point. If past the intersection, continue the approach, and land prior to the intersection. If larger aircraft rotates prior to the intersection, avoid flight below the larger aircraft’s flightpath. Abandon the approach unless a landing is ensured well before reaching the intersection. f. Departing behind a large aircraft—Note the larger aircraft’s rotation point and rotate prior to the larger aircraft’s rotation point. Continue climbing above the larger aircraft’s climb path until turning clear of the larger aircraft’s wake. Avoid subsequent headings that will cross below and behind a larger aircraft. g. Intersection takeoffs, same runway—Be alert to adjacent larger aircraft operations, especially of your runway. If intersection takeoff clearance is received, avoid subsequent heading which will cross below a larger aircraft’s path. h. Departing or landing after a larger aircraft executing a low approach, missed approach or touch-and-go landing—Vortices settle and move laterally near the ground. Because of this, the vortex hazard may exist along the runway and in your flight path after a larger aircraft has executed a low approach, missed approach or a touch-and-go landing, particularly in light quartering wind conditions. You should ensure that an interval of at least 2 minutes has elapsed before your takeoff or landing. i. En route VFR (thousand-foot altitude plus 500 feet)—Avoid flight below and behind a large aircraft’s path. If a larger aircraft is observed above or on the same track (meeting or overtaking) adjust your position laterally, preferably upwind. Remember: Acceptance of instructions from ATC is an acknowledgment that the pilot will ensure safe takeoff and landing intervals and accept the responsibility for providing wake turbulence separation.
key_reference_points: Landing behind larger aircraft: stay at or above their flight path, land BEYOND their touchdown point
Departing behind larger aircraft: rotate BEFORE their rotation point, climb ABOVE their climb path
After larger aircraft low approach/missed approach/touch-and-go: wait at least 2 MINUTES before takeoff or landing
Parallel runways closer than 2,500 feet: consider vortex drift to your runway
En route: avoid flight below and behind large aircraft, position laterally UPWIND if possible
Accepting ATC instructions means pilot accepts responsibility for wake turbulence separation

questions.question: What are several examples of illusions that may lead to landing errors? (AIM 8-1-5) Runway width illusion—A narrower-than-usual runway can create the illusion that the aircraft is at a higher altitude than it actually is. The pilot who does not recognize this illusion will fly a lower approach, with the risk of striking objects along the approach path or landing short. A wider-than-usual runway can have the opposite effect, with the risk of leveling out high and landing hard or overshooting the runway. Runway and terrain slopes illusion—An upsloping runway, upsloping terrain, or both, can create the illusion that the aircraft is at a higher altitude than it actually is. The pilot who does not recognize this illusion will fly a lower approach. A downsloping runway, downsloping approach terrain, or both, can have the opposite effect. Featureless terrain illusion—An absence of ground features, as when landing over water, darkened areas, and terrain made featureless by snow, can create the illusion that the aircraft is at a higher altitude than it actually is. The pilot who does not recognize this illusion will fly a lower approach. Atmospheric illusions—Rain on the windscreen can create the illusion of greater height, and atmospheric haze can create the illusion of being at a greater distance from the runway. The pilot who does not recognize these illusions will fly a lower approach.
key_reference_points: Narrow runway = illusion of higher altitude = dangerously low approach
Wide runway = illusion of lower altitude = risk of landing hard or overshooting
Upsloping runway/terrain = illusion of higher altitude = fly lower approach
Downsloping runway/terrain = illusion of lower altitude = fly higher approach
Featureless terrain (water, snow, darkness) = illusion of higher altitude = dangerously low approach
Rain on windscreen and haze create illusions of greater height/distance = lower approach

questions.question: The acronym “LAHSO” refers to what specific air traffic control procedure? (AIM 4-3-11) LAHSO is an acronym for “land and hold short operations.” At controlled airports, ATC may clear a pilot to land and hold short of an intersecting runway, an intersecting taxiway, or some other designated point on a runway. Pilots may accept such a clearance provided that the pilot-in-command determines the aircraft can safely land and stop within the available landing distance (ALD). Student pilots or pilots not familiar with LAHSO should not participate in the program. Pilots are expected to decline a LAHSO clearance if they determine it will compromise safety or if weather is below basic VFR conditions (a minimum ceiling of 1,000 feet and 3 SM visibility).
key_reference_points: LAHSO = Land And Hold Short Operations
Pilot must determine aircraft can safely land and stop within Available Landing Distance (ALD)
Student pilots should NOT participate in LAHSO
Pilots unfamiliar with LAHSO should decline the clearance
Decline LAHSO if weather is below basic VFR (ceiling < 1,000 ft or visibility < 3 SM)
LAHSO clearance can always be declined if safety is compromised

questions.question: Where can available landing distance (ALD) data be found? (AIM 4- 3-11) ALD data are published in the special notices section of the Chart Supplement U.S. and in the U.S. Terminal Procedures Publications. Controllers will also provide ALD data upon request.
key_reference_points: ALD data found in Chart Supplement U.S. (special notices section)
ALD data found in U.S. Terminal Procedures Publications
Controllers will provide ALD data upon pilot request

questions.question: Discuss recommended collision avoidance procedures and considerations in the following situations. (AIM 4-4-15)
questions.answer: a. Before takeoff—Prior to taxiing onto a runway or landing area in preparation for takeoff, pilots should scan the approach area for possible landing traffic, executing appropriate maneuvers to provide a clear view of the approach areas. b. Climbs and descents—During climbs and descents in flight conditions that permit visual detection of other traffic, pilots should execute gentle banks left and right at a frequency that allows continuous visual scanning of the airspace. c. Straight and level—During sustained periods of straight-and-level flight, a pilot should execute appropriate clearing procedures at periodic intervals. d. Traffic patterns—Entries into traffic patterns while descending should be avoided. e. Traffic at VOR sites—Due to converging traffic, sustained vigilance should be maintained in the vicinity of VORs and intersections. f. Training operations—Vigilance should be maintained and clearing turns should be made prior to a practice maneuver. During instruction, the pilot should be asked to verbalize the clearing procedures (call out clear “left, right, above, and below”). High-wing and low-wing aircraft have their respective blind spots. High-wing aircraft should momentarily raise the wing in the direction of the intended turn and look for traffic prior to commencing the turn. Low-wing aircraft should momentarily lower the wing.
key_reference_points: Before takeoff: scan approach area for landing traffic before taxiing onto runway
Climbs/descents: execute gentle banks left and right to scan airspace
Straight and level: perform clearing procedures at periodic intervals
Traffic patterns: avoid descending entries into patterns
VOR sites: maintain vigilance due to converging traffic
Training: perform clearing turns before maneuvers; verbalize 'clear left, right, above, below'
High-wing aircraft: raise wing in direction of turn before turning to check blind spot
Low-wing aircraft: lower wing momentarily to check blind spot before turning

questions.question: Where should you look for drones in your area? (14 CFR Part 107, AIM 7-5-5) Unmanned aircraft systems (UAS, also called “drones”) cannot operate in controlled airspace without obtaining a waiver from the FAA. However, it is possible I might see drones in my area. Drones must fly below 400 feet AGL, can only operate in daylight hours, and must stay clear of clouds.
key_reference_points: UAS cannot operate in controlled airspace without FAA waiver
Drones must fly below 400 feet AGL
Drones can only operate during daylight hours
Drones must stay clear of clouds
Reference: 14 CFR Part 107 and AIM 7-5-5

questions.question: What are three major areas that contribute to runway incursions? (FAA-H-8083-3)
questions.answer: a. Communications—misunderstanding the given clearance; failure to communicate effectively b. Airport knowledge—failure to navigate the airport correctly; unable to interpret airport signage c. Cockpit procedures for maintaining orientation—failure to maintain situational awareness
key_reference_points: Communications: misunderstanding clearances or failure to communicate effectively
Airport knowledge: failure to navigate correctly or interpret airport signage
Cockpit procedures: failure to maintain situational awareness and orientation
These three areas are the major contributors to runway incursions per FAA-H-8083-3

questions.question: Preflight planning for taxi operations should be an integral part of the pilot’s flight planning process. What information should this include? (AC 91-73)
questions.answer: a. Review and understand airport signage, markings and lighting. b. Review the airport diagram, planned taxi route, and identify any “hot spots.” c. Review the latest airfield NOTAMs and ATIS (if available) for taxiway/runway closures, construction activity, etc. d. Conduct a pre-taxi/pre-landing briefing that includes the expected/assigned taxi route and any hold short lines and restrictions based on ATIS information or previous experience at the airport. e. Plan for critical times and locations on the taxi route (complex intersections, crossing runways, etc.). f. Plan to complete as many aircraft checklist items as possible prior to taxi.
key_reference_points: Review airport signage, markings, and lighting before taxi
Study airport diagram and planned taxi route, noting hot spots
Check NOTAMs and ATIS for taxiway/runway closures and construction
Conduct pre-taxi briefing including expected route and hold short restrictions
Identify critical locations: complex intersections and runway crossings
Complete as many checklist items as possible before beginning taxi

questions.question: What is an airport “hot spot”? (FAA-H-8083-16) A “hot spot” is a runway safety-related problem area or intersection on an airport. Typically, hot spots are complex or confusing taxiway–taxiway or taxiway–runway intersections. A lack of visibility may exist at certain points and/or the tower may be unable to see those particular intersections. Pilots should be increasingly vigilant when approaching and taxiing through these intersections.
key_reference_points: Hot spots are runway safety-related problem areas on an airport
Typically located at complex taxiway-taxiway or taxiway-runway intersections
May have limited visibility for pilots or tower controllers
Depicted on airport diagrams with specific symbology
Require increased pilot vigilance when approaching and taxiing through

questions.question: Why is use of “sterile cockpit” procedures important when conducting taxi operations? (AC 91-73) Pilots must be able to focus on their duties without being distracted by non- flight-related matters unrelated to the safe and proper operation of the aircraft. Refraining from nonessential activities during ground operations is essential. Passengers should be briefed on the importance of minimizing conversations and questions during taxi as well as on arrival, from the time landing preparations begin until the aircraft is safely parked.
key_reference_points: Sterile cockpit means no nonessential activities during critical phases
Applies during taxi operations, not just flight phases
Pilots must focus solely on safe aircraft operation during taxi
Brief passengers to minimize conversations during taxi and landing
Sterile cockpit period on arrival: from landing prep until aircraft is parked

questions.question: When should a pilot request “progressive” taxi instructions? (AIM 4- 3-18) If the pilot is unfamiliar with the airport or for any reason confusion exists as to the correct taxi routing, a request may be made for progressive taxi instructions, which include step-by-step routing directions.
key_reference_points: Request progressive taxi when unfamiliar with the airport
Request progressive taxi when any confusion exists about correct taxi routing
Progressive taxi provides step-by-step routing directions from ATC
Reference: AIM 4-3-18

questions.question: After completing your pre-taxi/pre-landing briefing of the taxi route you “expect” to receive, ATC calls and gives you a different route. What potential pitfall is common in this situation? (AC 91-73) A common pitfall of pre-taxi and pre-landing planning is setting expectations and then receiving different instructions from ATC. Pilots need to follow the instructions that they actually receive, and not the ones they expect to receive. Short term memory is of limited duration.
key_reference_points: Common pitfall: expecting one route but receiving different instructions from ATC
Always follow instructions actually received, not instructions expected
Short-term memory is limited duration - easy to revert to expected route
Pre-planning creates expectations that may conflict with actual clearances
Reference: AC 91-73

questions.question: Why is it a good idea to write down taxi instructions, especially at larger or unfamiliar airports? (AC 91-73) Writing down taxi instructions, especially complex instructions, can reduce a pilot’s vulnerability to forgetting part of the instructions and provides a reference for read-back of instructions to ATC. It can also be used as a means of reconfirming the taxi route and any restrictions at any time during taxi operations.
key_reference_points: Writing down instructions reduces vulnerability to forgetting parts of complex clearances
Written instructions provide reference for accurate read-back to ATC
Written record allows reconfirmation of taxi route at any time during taxi
Especially important at larger or unfamiliar airports
Written instructions help verify any restrictions received
Reference: AC 91-73

questions.question: When issued taxi instructions to an assigned takeoff runway, are you automatically authorized to cross any runway that intersects your taxi route? (AIM 4-3-18) No; Aircraft must receive a runway crossing clearance for each runway that their taxi route crosses. When assigned a takeoff runway, ATC will first specify the runway, issue taxi instructions, and state any hold short instructions or runway crossing clearances if the taxi route will cross a runway. When issuing taxi instructions to any point other than an assigned takeoff runway, ATC will specify the point to which to taxi, issue taxi instructions, and state any hold short instructions or runway crossing clearances if the taxi route will cross a runway. ATC is required to obtain a read back from the pilot of all runway hold short instructions.
key_reference_points: You are NOT automatically authorized to cross any runway - explicit crossing clearance required for each runway
ATC must issue specific runway crossing clearances if taxi route crosses any runway
ATC will state hold short instructions or crossing clearances when assigning takeoff runway
Pilot must read back all runway hold short instructions to ATC
Never cross a runway hold short line without explicit ATC clearance

questions.question: When receiving taxi instructions from a controller, pilots should always read back what information? (AIM 4-3-18)
questions.answer: a. The runway assignment. b. Any clearance to enter a specific runway. c. Any instruction to hold short of a specific runway or line up and wait.
key_reference_points: Always read back the runway assignment
Always read back any clearance to enter a specific runway
Always read back any instruction to hold short of a specific runway
Always read back any line up and wait instruction
If uncertain about any taxi instruction, ask ATC for clarification

questions.question: What are some recommended practices that can assist a pilot in maintaining situational awareness during taxi operations? (AC 91-73)
questions.answer: a. A current airport diagram should be available for immediate reference during taxi. b. Monitor ATC instructions/clearances issued to other aircraft for the “big picture.” c. Focus attention outside the cockpit while taxiing. d. Use all available resources (airport diagrams, airport signs, markings, lighting, and ATC) to keep the aircraft on its assigned taxi route. e. Cross-reference heading indicator to ensure turns are being made in the correct direction and that you’re on the assigned taxi route. f. Prior to crossing any hold short line, visually check for conflicting traffic; verbalize “clear left, clear right.” g. Be alert for other aircraft with similar call signs on the frequency. h. Understand and follow all ATC instructions and if in doubt—Ask!
key_reference_points: Keep current airport diagram readily available during taxi operations
Monitor ATC instructions to other aircraft for situational awareness
Focus attention outside cockpit while taxiing - heads up
Cross-reference heading indicator during turns to verify correct taxi route
Before crossing any hold short line, visually verify clear left and clear right
When in doubt about any ATC instruction - ASK for clarification

questions.question: How can a pilot use aircraft exterior lighting to enhance situational awareness and safety during airport surface operations? (AC 91-73; SAFO) To the extent possible and consistent with aircraft equipment, operating limitations, and pilot procedures, pilots should illuminate exterior lights as follows:
questions.answer: a. Engines running—Turn on the rotating beacon whenever an engine is running. b. Taxiing—Prior to commencing taxi, turn on navigation/position lights and anti-collision lights. c. Crossing a runway—All exterior lights should be illuminated when crossing a runway. d. Entering the departure runway for takeoff—All exterior lights (except landing lights) should be on to make your aircraft more conspicuous to aircraft on final and ATC. e. Cleared for takeoff—All exterior lights, including takeoff/landing lights should be on.
key_reference_points: Rotating beacon ON whenever an engine is running
Navigation/position lights and anti-collision lights ON before taxiing
ALL exterior lights ON when crossing any runway
All exterior lights EXCEPT landing lights ON when entering departure runway
ALL exterior lights INCLUDING landing lights ON when cleared for takeoff
Purpose is to maximize aircraft conspicuity to other aircraft and ATC
questions.note: If you see an aircraft in takeoff position on a runway with landing lights ON, that aircraft has most likely received its takeoff clearance and will be departing immediately.

questions.question: During calm or nearly calm wind conditions, at an airport without an operating control tower, a pilot should be aware of what potentially hazardous situations? (AC 91-73) Aircraft may be landing and/or taking off on more than one runway at the airport. Also, aircraft may be using an instrument approach procedure to runways other than the runway in use for VFR operations. The instrument approach runway may intersect the VFR runway. It is also possible that an instrument arrival may be made to the opposite end of the runway from which a takeoff is being made.
key_reference_points: Calm winds allow aircraft to use multiple runways simultaneously
IFR aircraft may be using instrument approaches to different runways than VFR traffic
Instrument approach runway may intersect the VFR runway in use
Opposite direction operations possible - IFR arrivals to opposite end of departure runway
Extra vigilance required at non-towered airports during calm wind conditions
Monitor CTAF for all traffic announcements, not just your runway

questions.question: You have just landed at a tower-controlled airport and missed your assigned taxiway for exiting the runway. Is it permissible for you to turn around on the runway and return to the exit taxiway? (AIM 4-3-20) No; At airports with an operating control tower, pilots should never stop or reverse course on the runway without first obtaining ATC approval.
key_reference_points: NEVER stop or reverse course on a runway at a towered airport without ATC approval
If you miss your exit taxiway, continue to the next available exit
Contact tower immediately if you need to reverse direction
ATC approval is REQUIRED before any reversal of direction on the runway
Reference: AIM 4-3-20

questions.question: When taxiing at a non-towered airport, what are several precautionary measures you should take prior to entering or crossing a runway? (AC 91-73) Listen on the appropriate frequency (CTAF) for inbound aircraft information and always scan the full length of the runway, including the final approach and departure paths, before entering or crossing the runway. Self-announce your position and intentions and remember that not all aircraft are radio- equipped.
key_reference_points: Monitor CTAF frequency for inbound traffic information before entering/crossing runway
Visually scan full runway length, final approach path, and departure path before proceeding
Self-announce position and intentions on CTAF
Remember not all aircraft have radios - visual scanning is essential
Reference: AC 91-73 for non-towered airport operations

questions.question: ATC has instructed you to line up and wait on the departure runway due to crossing traffic on an intersecting taxiway. What is considered a reasonable amount of time to wait for a takeoff clearance before calling ATC? (AIM 5-2-4; SAFO 11004) FAA analysis of accidents and incidents involving aircraft holding in position indicate that two minutes or more elapsed between the time the instruction was issued to line up and wait and the resulting event (for example, land-over or go-around). If you have been holding in position on the runway for more than 90 seconds, or you see or hear a potential conflict, contact ATC immediately.
key_reference_points: 90 seconds is the threshold - contact ATC if holding longer on runway
FAA data shows incidents typically occur when holding 2+ minutes
Contact ATC immediately if you see or hear any potential conflict
Reference: AIM 5-2-4 and SAFO 11004 for line up and wait procedures
Never assume ATC remembers you are holding in position

questions.question: What are several actions you can take to enhance aircraft security? (TSA)
questions.answer: a. Always lock your aircraft. b. Keep track of door/ignition keys and don’t leave keys in unattended aircraft. c. Use secondary locks (prop, tie down, throttle, and wheel locks) or aircraft disabler if available. d. Lock hangar when unattended.
key_reference_points: Always lock aircraft doors when unattended
Never leave keys in unattended aircraft
Use secondary locks: prop locks, tie-down locks, throttle locks, wheel locks
Consider aircraft disabler devices if available
Lock hangar doors when unattended
Reference: TSA aircraft security guidelines

questions.question: What type of airport security procedures should you review regularly to prevent unauthorized access to aircraft at your airport? (FSSAT)
questions.answer: a. Limitations on ramp access to people other than instructors and students b. Standards for securing aircraft on the ramp c. Securing access to aircraft keys at all times d. New auxiliary security items for aircraft (prop locks, throttle locks, locking tie downs) e. After-hours or weekend access procedures
key_reference_points: Ramp access should be limited to authorized instructors and students only
Aircraft must be properly secured on the ramp per established standards
Aircraft keys must be secured at all times - never left in aircraft
Auxiliary security devices include prop locks, throttle locks, and locking tie downs
Know your airport's specific after-hours and weekend access procedures
Review FSSAT (Flight School Security Awareness Training) regularly

questions.question: Give some examples of what you would consider suspicious activity at an airport. (TSA)
questions.answer: a. Aircraft with unusual modifications (such as modified N-numbers) or activity b. Unfamiliar persons loitering for extended periods in the vicinity of parked aircraft c. Anyone making threats d. Events or circumstances that do not fit the pattern of lawful, normal activity at an airport e. Pilots appearing to be under the control of others
key_reference_points: Aircraft with unusual modifications or altered/modified N-numbers
Unfamiliar persons loitering near parked aircraft for extended periods
Anyone making threats of any kind
Activity that doesn't fit normal, lawful airport operations
Pilots who appear to be under duress or control of others
TSA provides guidance on recognizing suspicious airport activity

questions.question: When witnessing suspicious or criminal activity, what are three basic ways for reporting the suspected activity? (TSA) If you determine that it’s safe, question the individual. If their response is unsatisfactory and they continue to act suspiciously:
questions.answer: a. Alert airport or FBO management. b. Contact local law enforcement if the activity poses an immediate threat to persons or property. c. Contact the 866-GA-SECURE hotline to document the reported event.
key_reference_points: First option: Alert airport or FBO management
Second option: Contact local law enforcement if immediate threat exists
Third option: Call 866-GA-SECURE hotline to document the event
If safe to do so, question the individual before escalating
Only escalate if their response is unsatisfactory and suspicious behavior continues
866-GA-SECURE is the TSA's dedicated general aviation security hotline

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 866-GA-SECURE phone number? (TSA) 866-GA-SECURE is a toll-free hotline operated by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) Security Operations Center. It is staffed 24/7 to take reports of suspicious or criminal activity occurring at general aviation airports. TSA personnel will document the reported activity, collect your personal contact numbers, and pass the information on to the appropriate regulatory office within the TSA.
key_reference_points: 866-GA-SECURE is a TSA-operated toll-free hotline
Available 24/7 for reporting suspicious activity at GA airports
Staffed by TSA Security Operations Center personnel
Reports are documented and forwarded to appropriate TSA regulatory offices
Callers should be prepared to provide personal contact information
questions.note: Calling 866-GA-SECURE will not dispatch local law enforcement. In the event of an immediate emergency, 911 or local law enforcement should be contacted first.

questions.question: What are several sources of information available to pilots interested in additional guidance on aviation security? (TSA; FSSAT) Security Guidelines for General Aviation Airports is a set of federally endorsed guidelines that offers an extensive list of options, ideas, and suggestions for the airport operator, sponsor, tenant and/or user to choose from when considering security enhancements for GA facilities. Flight School Security Awareness Training for Aircraft and Simulators is an online training course designed to raise the general security awareness levels of employees working in the flight training industry.
key_reference_points: Security Guidelines for General Aviation Airports - federally endorsed guidelines for GA security enhancements
Flight School Security Awareness Training (FSSAT) - online course for flight training industry employees
TSA provides resources for airport operators, sponsors, tenants, and users
FSSAT focuses on raising security awareness levels in flight training

questions.question: Prior to departure, what items should you brief your passengers on? (FAA-H-8083-3)
key_reference_points: Use SAFETY briefing acronym: Seatbelts, Air vents, Fire extinguisher, Exit doors, Traffic/Talking, Yoke/Your questions
Brief passengers on seatbelt operation and when to keep fastened
Explain door operation and emergency exit procedures
Identify location and operation of fire extinguisher
Establish sterile cockpit rules - no talking during critical phases
Instruct passengers not to touch flight controls

questions.question: When navigating by VOR, when will you have “reverse sensing?” (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Reverse sensing occurs when flying TO a station with the OBS set to the reciprocal (FROM) heading
Reverse sensing occurs when flying FROM a station with the OBS set to the TO heading
CDI deflection will be opposite to the actual position relative to the selected radial
Always ensure the TO/FROM indicator matches your intended direction of flight
Correcting toward the needle during reverse sensing takes you further off course

questions.question: If your Mode C transponder fails while en route, can you continue flight into Class B or Class C airspace? (AIM 4-1-20)
key_reference_points: ATC may authorize deviation from transponder requirements on a continuing basis
Request must be made at least 1 hour before proposed operation
Pilot must request deviation from ATC facility having jurisdiction over the airspace
Class B airspace: immediate deviation requests go to the ATC facility having jurisdiction
Class C airspace: ATC may approve operations without Mode C on a case-by-case basis
Without ATC authorization, you cannot continue into Class B or C airspace with failed Mode C

questions.question: Is a mode C transponder required for flight over Class C airspace if operating below 10,000 ft. MSL? (AIM 4-1-20)
key_reference_points: Mode C transponder IS required above Class C airspace up to 10,000 ft MSL
The Mode C veil extends from surface to 10,000 ft MSL within the lateral boundaries
14 CFR 91.215 requires Mode C within and above Class C airspace
This requirement applies within the lateral limits of Class C surface area
Exception: aircraft without an electrical system may request deviation from ATC

questions.question: During preflight planning and while en route, how can pilots mitigate the risk involved with deteriorating weather conditions at their destination? (FAA-H-8083-2)
key_reference_points: Check weather at alternate airports along the route before departure
Obtain updated weather briefings from FSS while en route
Establish personal minimums for ceiling, visibility, and crosswind limits
Have predetermined diversion points and alternate airports identified
Use the PAVE checklist (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures) to assess risk
Be willing to divert, delay, or cancel if conditions deteriorate below personal minimums

questions.question: Where can a pilot find information on the location of the nearest VOT testing station? (AIM 1-1-4)
key_reference_points: VOT locations are published in the Chart Supplement (formerly A/FD)
AIM Section 1-1-4 covers VOR receiver accuracy checks
VOT transmits a 360° radial in all directions for testing
Allowable error: ±4° for ground VOT check
VOT check must be logged with date, place, bearing error, and signature

questions.question: Discuss the various factors a pilot should consider when making a Go/No-Go decision for a particular flight. (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Use PAVE checklist: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures
Pilot: fitness, currency, proficiency, fatigue, stress, medications
Aircraft: airworthiness, performance capability, fuel range, required inspections current
enVironment: weather conditions, terrain, airspace, airport conditions, NOTAMs
External pressures: time constraints, passenger expectations, get-there-itis
Apply personal minimums that exceed FAA regulatory minimums

questions.question: How will you position your aircraft’s flight control surfaces while taxiing in the following conditions: quartering tailwind, quartering headwind? (FAA-H-8083-3)
key_reference_points: Quartering headwind: aileron UP into the wind, elevator neutral
Quartering tailwind: aileron DOWN on the side wind is coming from, elevator DOWN (dive away)
Memory aid: 'Climb into headwind, dive away from tailwind'
Control positions prevent wind from lifting wings or tail
Headwind component: controls positioned as if climbing into the wind
Tailwind component: controls positioned as if diving away from the wind

questions.question: Demonstrate the following hand signals utilized by a lineman when directing you to or from a ramp: (AIM 4-3-25)
questions.answer: a. All clear (OK) b. Start engine c. Pull chocks d. Come ahead e. Left turn f. Right turn g. Slow down h. Stop i. Insert chocks j. Cut engines k. Emergency stop
key_reference_points: All clear/OK: Arms extended, palms facing out, thumbs up or arms in front forming an O
Start engine: Circular motion with right hand at head level, left arm pointing to engine
Pull chocks: Hands pulled apart horizontally
Come ahead: Arms extended, palms facing inward, beckoning motion
Left turn: Right arm extended, left arm sweeping overhead toward left
Right turn: Left arm extended, right arm sweeping overhead toward right
Slow down: Arms extended, palms down, moving up and down
Stop: Arms crossed overhead
Cut engines: Hand/arm drawn across throat in cutting motion
Emergency stop: Arms extended, waving rapidly

questions.question: What two factors should be considered when evaluating the type of survival equipment to carry for a flight over uninhabited terrain? (AIM 6-2-6)
key_reference_points: Factor 1: Geographic area being flown over (desert, mountains, water, arctic)
Factor 2: Season of the year (weather conditions, temperature extremes)
Survival kit contents should match terrain type (water survival vs cold weather vs desert)
Consider signaling devices, water, shelter, and first aid appropriate to environment
File flight plan and inform someone of route when flying over uninhabited terrain

questions.question: Why is a postflight inspection recommended, and what are you looking for during that inspection? (FAA-H-8083-3)
key_reference_points: Postflight inspection detects damage or wear that occurred during flight
Check for fluid leaks (oil, fuel, hydraulic)
Inspect tires for wear, cuts, or proper inflation
Look for bird strikes, dents, or structural damage
Check control surfaces for damage or loose components
Document any squawks for maintenance before next flight

questions.question: Why do the Class E airspace cloud clearance and visibility requirements change above 10,000 feet? (FAA Safety ALC-25)
key_reference_points: Above 10,000 feet MSL, aircraft speeds increase significantly (no 250 knot speed limit)
Higher closure rates require greater visibility to see-and-avoid traffic
Cloud clearances increase to 1 SM horizontal, 1,000 ft above/below (from 500/1000/2000)
Visibility requirement increases to 5 SM (from 3 SM below 10,000 ft)
These requirements only apply when above 10,000 MSL AND more than 2,500 AGL
Designed to provide adequate time to spot and avoid faster-moving aircraft
